finding.summary: The axi_lite_interface module performs no privilege, lock, or accountability check before processing AXI read/write transactions. Access-control inputs (reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, debug_mode_i) are received by the wrapper modules but are not forwarded to or enforced within axi_lite_interface, which blindly accepts and completes any well-formed AXI request, making the register-lock and account-control mechanisms ineffective at the bus interface layer.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface accepts axi_req_i directly and drives en_o/we_o/address_o without any check against privilege level, register-lock, or account-control signals. The module port list contains no reglk_ctrl, acct_ctrl, or debug_mode inputs whatsoever.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI bus interface module that fans out to all security-sensitive peripherals (RSA, RNG, PKT) contains no permission guard inputs or guard logic.

evidence.description: The WRITE state unconditionally asserts en_o=1 and we_o=1 whenever w_valid is high, with no conditional check on any lock or privilege signal. Similarly, the READ state unconditionally asserts r_valid.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard logic inside axi_lite_interface that could enforce reglk or acct_ctrl before completing a transaction.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i [7:0] and acct_ctrl_i but these are not passed to the instantiated axi_lite_interface_i; the interface is wired only to clk_i, rst_ni, axi_req_i, axi_resp_o, address, en_acct, we, wdata, and rdata.
evidence.supports_claim: Lock and account-control signals exist in the wrapper but are not plumbed into the AXI interface that actually generates the en/we strobes.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper similarly receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i but the axi_lite_interface instantiation (lines 69-75) connects only clk_i, rst_ni, axi_req_i, axi_resp_o, and the derived address/en/we/data signals without any permission signal.
evidence.supports_claim: For the RNG peripheral the same pattern holds: permission inputs are present in the wrapper but absent from the bus interface, leaving access unguarded at the point where en/we are generated.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i but the axi_lite_interface instantiation (lines 46-51) receives none of these, confirming the same pattern for the RSA peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: RSA access-control inputs are not forwarded to the AXI interface layer where read/write enables are produced.

evidence.description: RSA wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl[13*8+7:13*8] and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][13], indicating the designer intended privilege-level enforcement for RSA register access.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege-level-indexed access control is intended for the RSA peripheral but is not enforced within axi_lite_interface where transactions are actually committed.

evidence.description: RNG wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl[10*8+7:10*8] and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][10], confirming intent for privilege-level enforcement that is not carried through to axi_lite_interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Same bypass issue exists for the RNG peripheral.

evidence.description: PKT wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8], confirming a register-lock scheme for the PKT (key storage) peripheral that is equally not enforced at the axi_lite_interface level.
evidence.supports_claim: Key-storage peripheral (PKT) register locks can be bypassed because axi_lite_interface never checks them.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface has no reglk_ctrl, acct_ctrl, or privilege-level port. Any AXI master that can form a valid aw_valid/w_valid handshake will successfully write to the protected peripheral registers regardless of lock state or privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard in the shared AXI interface module allows bypass of all register-lock and account-control protections for RSA, RNG, and PKT peripherals.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design uses a shared axi_lite_interface module to decode AXI transactions and produce register-enable (en_o) and write-enable (we_o) strobes for several security-sensitive peripherals (RSA, RNG, PKT/key-storage). Each wrapper module receives register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) and account-control (acct_ctrl_i) inputs that are indexed by the current privilege level. However, none of these permission signals are connected to axi_lite_interface. The interface state machine unconditionally asserts en_o and we_o for any valid AXI write handshake and unconditionally serves read data for any valid AXI read. Because the en/we signals originate inside axi_lite_interface and the permission checks (if any exist further downstream in the wrapper) occur after the transaction is already committed by the interface, the lock mechanism is at best delayed and at worst entirely ineffective at preventing unauthorized access.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or adversarial AXI master can read from and write to the RSA key registers, RNG seed/polynomial registers, and PKT fuse/key registers without satisfying any register-lock or privilege-level check. This enables extraction of private keys, seed material, and fuse data, as well as corruption of cryptographic parameters (primes, polynomials, seeds) used by the RSA and RNG engines.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of rsa_wrapper, rng_wrapper, and pkt_wrapper beyond the AXI interface instantiation is not shown; it is possible that en_acct (which is wired to en_o from axi_lite_interface) is subsequently gated by reglk/acct_ctrl before reaching the actual register file. However, the snippets clearly show that by the time en_o is asserted the AXI handshake (including the write data channel) has already been consumed, so even a downstream gate would be too late to prevent the transaction from being recorded.

finding.summary: The axi_lite_interface module directly passes data_i to axi_resp_o.r.data without any register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) or access-control (acct_ctrl_i) check. Sensitive peripheral data from RSA, RNG, and PKT wrappers flows unconditionally through the AXI read response channel regardless of the lock state set by reglk_ctrl_i.

evidence.description: Line 42: `assign axi_resp_o.r.data = data_i;` is a direct, unconditional continuous assignment. No reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i signal is present in axi_lite_interface; the module has no knowledge of these protection signals at all.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive read data (RSA msg_out, RNG seed/poly/rand, PKT fuse key material) is forwarded to the AXI read response bus without any permission or lock check.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs for access control, and its internal rdata signal (which carries RSA msg_out and other sensitive RSA register data) is wired directly to axi_lite_interface's data_i port (edge:connects:106). The protection signals are used elsewhere in rsa_wrapper but not enforced at the AXI response path through axi_lite_interface.
evidence.supports_claim: RSA output data (including decrypted message) reaches the AXI response channel without lock verification inside axi_lite_interface.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i for access control. Its rdata signal (carrying seed, polynomial, random number values) is wired to axi_lite_interface's data_i (edge:connects:78). The comment on line 46 notes 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode', indicating sensitivity.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG seed and internal state data reaches the AXI response channel without lock verification inside axi_lite_interface.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, and its rdata (carrying fuse/key material) is wired to axi_lite_interface's data_i (edge:connects:41). The PKT module handles fuse-based key storage (pkey_loc signal).
evidence.supports_claim: Fuse key material from PKT wrapper reaches the AXI response channel without lock verification inside axi_lite_interface.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates rsa_wrapper with reglk_ctrl[13*8+7:13*8] and acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][13], but these signals go to rsa_wrapper ports, not into axi_lite_interface. The axi_lite_interface sub-instance inside rsa_wrapper has no connection to these control signals.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock and account-control guards are plumbed into the wrapper but are absent from the axi_lite_interface that actually drives the read response data.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates rng_wrapper with reglk_ctrl[10*8+7:10*8] and acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][10]. Same pattern: protection signals do not reach axi_lite_interface.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG register-lock and privilege controls are not enforced at the AXI read data path.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates pkt_wrapper with reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8]. Same structural gap applies.
evidence.supports_claim: PKT register-lock control is not enforced at the AXI read data path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The axi_lite_interface module contains `assign axi_resp_o.r.data = data_i` (SNIP-0004, line 42), which is an unconditional pass-through of whatever data is provided by the parent wrapper. The RSA, RNG, and PKT wrappers each have reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i ports that are supposed to gate access to sensitive registers, but the axi_lite_interface sub-module—which drives the actual AXI read response—has no such inputs and performs no locking checks. Therefore, even when reglk_ctrl_i locks are asserted at the wrapper level, any read transaction that reaches axi_lite_interface's READ state will still return the data_i value unconditionally. The protection logic inside each wrapper may gate the en/we signals controlling the register file, but the rdata output is combinatorially connected to data_i of axi_lite_interface, and the response assignment bypasses any wrapper-level guard.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or unauthorized AXI master can read sensitive data including RSA plaintext/ciphertext outputs, RNG seeds and polynomial coefficients, and PKT fuse-based key material by issuing read transactions to the peripheral address space even when register-lock controls are intended to prevent such access. This constitutes a sensitive data readback vulnerability that could expose cryptographic key material.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets for the wrapper bodies (e.g., the actual rdata mux logic inside rsa_wrapper, rng_wrapper, pkt_wrapper that might zero-out rdata when reglk_ctrl_i is set) are not fully shown. It is possible that each wrapper drives rdata to zero when locked, which would mitigate the axi_lite_interface pass-through. However, the structural absence of any guard in axi_lite_interface itself, combined with the unconditional assign statement, represents a design pattern where the protection depends entirely on upstream combinational zeroing rather than an enforced gate at the response interface.

finding.summary: fuse_mem continuously exposes sensitive cryptographic key hashes (jtag_hash_o, ikey_hash_o, okey_hash_o) as combinational outputs with no access control, privilege check, or lock guard. The keys are hardcoded in the RTL and permanently driven onto wires visible to the rest of the SoC.

evidence.description: fuse_mem stores hardcoded 256-bit JTAG, ikey, and okey hash values directly in a const ROM array initialised with literal hex constants. These are security-critical secrets (JTAG authentication hashes and integrity keys).
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse memory holds sensitive cryptographic material that should be access-controlled.

evidence.description: Lines 137-139 unconditionally assign jtag_hash_o, okey_hash_o, and ikey_hash_o directly from the ROM with pure combinational assigns and no enable, privilege, lock, or lifecycle gate: 'assign jtag_hash_o = {mem[JTAG_OFFSET-1],...}; assign okey_hash_o = ...; assign ikey_hash_o = ...;'. req_i is only used to latch the read address for rdata_o; it is not consulted for the hash outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: All three 256-bit secret key hashes are permanently driven onto output wires regardless of any request, privilege level, lock register, or lifecycle state.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates fuse_mem without passing a reset, lock, or privilege signal. The hash outputs are wired directly to internal signals jtag_hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash with no intervening gating logic visible at the instantiation site.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no access-control wrapper applied at the integration boundary; the hashes flow freely into riscv_peripherals.

evidence.description: jtag_hash, ikey_hash, and okey_hash are declared as plain logic signals in riscv_peripherals with no protection attribute or gating. jtag_unlock is declared but no code in the provided snippets gates the hash outputs on it.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no permission state that blocks readout of the key hashes after reset or during normal operation.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper implements reglk_ctrl_i guards only for pkey_loc (bits 4-6) and fuse_rdata_i (bit 6), but fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o (cases 0 and 1) have no lock check. The hash outputs from fuse_mem are not gated here at all because they bypass pkt_wrapper entirely.
evidence.supports_claim: Even the partial register-lock logic present in pkt_wrapper does not cover the jtag/ikey/okey hash outputs, confirming there is no guard on the sensitive hash path.

finding.reasoning_summary: fuse_mem.sv exposes three 256-bit cryptographic hashes (JTAG auth hash, ikey hash, okey hash) through permanent combinational assigns (lines 137-139) that are active at all times, independent of req_i, any lock register, privilege level, lifecycle state, or debug enable. The module interface includes no such control input for these outputs. The integration in riscv_peripherals (SNIP-0003) connects these outputs to unguarded internal wires. pkt_wrapper does implement reglk_ctrl_i guards for some registers but not for the hash signals, which do not even pass through pkt_wrapper. The result is that any agent that can observe the internal wires jtag_hash, ikey_hash, or okey_hash—or that reads the signals downstream—obtains the full 256-bit secrets without any authentication or permission check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to any peripheral bus master, debug interface, or observation of the riscv_peripherals signals can read the JTAG authentication secret, the integrity key (ikey), and the owner key (okey) in cleartext at any time during device operation, bypassing all authentication based on those secrets and enabling arbitrary JTAG unlock and key-material disclosure.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The downstream consumers of jtag_hash, ikey_hash, and okey_hash inside riscv_peripherals are not shown in the provided snippets, so it is not possible to confirm whether a guard exists further along the datapath. However, the fuse_mem module itself provides no gating, and the instantiation in SNIP-0003 shows no additional control signals are connected.

finding.summary: fuse_mem raw read data (rdata_o) and fuse addresses 0 and 1 (fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o) are returned to AXI bus without any reglk_ctrl gate, while only addresses 2-4 are selectively guarded, allowing unauthorized readback of fuse memory content and control registers.

evidence.description: fuse_mem contains hardcoded secret key material (jtag_hash, okey_hash, ikey_hash) and exposes rdata_o as a direct read-out of the fuse array mem[] with no access control gate inside the module itself.
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse memory holds sensitive cryptographic hash values and allows arbitrary address-indexed reads via rdata_o with no permission check.

evidence.description: In the read-side always block of pkt_wrapper, address case 0 returns fuse_req_o and case 1 returns fuse_addr_o without any reglk_ctrl_i check. Case 2, 3 check reglk_ctrl_i[4] and [5] respectively, and case 4 checks reglk_ctrl_i[6] before returning fuse_rdata_i. The default path (line 109-110) also returns 32'b0 without a lock check but note that the fuse_rdata_i read path at line 96 executes unconditionally whenever 'en' is set before the case statement is evaluated, potentially leaking data.
evidence.supports_claim: Register addresses 0 and 1 (fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o) are readable without any reglk_ctrl guard, and the initial assignment rdata = fuse_rdata_i at line 96 occurs before address-specific lock checks.

evidence.description: At line 96, rdata is unconditionally set to fuse_rdata_i whenever 'en' is asserted, before the case statement applies reglk_ctrl-gated overrides for specific addresses. For any address not matching cases 0-4, the default path at line 109 only conditionally zeroes the output; fuse_rdata_i value set at line 96 is the default return unless overridden. This means fuse memory data can be read back without the lock check that was intended to gate address 4.
evidence.supports_claim: The unconditional assignment rdata = fuse_rdata_i at line 96 before reglk_ctrl checks at lines 103/105/107 means that for unhandled addresses the lock is bypassed, and fuse memory content leaks to the AXI read response.

evidence.description: Address case 0 (line 99) returns fuse_req_o and case 1 (line 101) returns fuse_addr_o with no reglk_ctrl_i bit check, while cases 2 and 3 use reglk_ctrl_i[4] and [5]. This asymmetry means an attacker can read control registers (req and addr state) freely, and can also infer/manipulate fuse access patterns.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing reglk_ctrl guard on address cases 0 and 1 allows unrestricted readback of fuse request and address registers.

evidence.description: fuse_mem module interface exposes rdata_o as a 32-bit output containing arbitrary fuse memory words indexed by addr_i, with the comment 'FUSE mem: Which have all the secure data'. The module has no internal access control.
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse memory is explicitly described as holding all secure data, making unauthorized readback a high-severity vulnerability.

evidence.description: fuse_mem is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with fuse_rdata connected directly back to pkt_wrapper's fuse_rdata_i, creating a direct path from fuse array contents to the AXI read response logic in pkt_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The data flow from fuse_mem.rdata_o to pkt_wrapper's AXI response is confirmed at the integration level.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8] (8 bits) is passed to pkt_wrapper as reglk_ctrl_i, but only bits [4], [5], and [6] are used to gate specific read cases; bits [0]-[3] and [7] are unused for fuse read protection.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl lock mechanism is partially applied; several fuse read paths remain ungated.

finding.reasoning_summary: The fuse_mem module stores sensitive cryptographic key material and provides direct readback via rdata_o. In pkt_wrapper, the AXI read path unconditionally loads rdata = fuse_rdata_i when 'en' is asserted (line 96), then selectively overrides this for specific address cases with reglk_ctrl checks (cases 2, 3, 4). However, cases 0 and 1 have no lock check, and any AXI address not matching cases 0-4 falls to the default which may return the pre-assigned fuse_rdata_i. This means fuse memory contents can be read without proper authorization by addressing unprotected cases or exploiting the unconditional pre-assignment.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access (e.g., a compromised software process at any privilege level that can reach the PKT peripheral MMIO space) can read arbitrary fuse memory words containing cryptographic key hashes (jtag_hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash) and other secure data by issuing reads to the unguarded address offsets or by exploiting the unconditional fuse_rdata_i assignment. This undermines the security guarantees of the key storage and potentially allows bypass of JTAG authentication.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact AXI address mapping for the PKT peripheral is not shown in the snippets, so the precise software-visible address range an attacker must target is not fully confirmed. Additionally, the 'acct_ctrl_i' gating on 'en' (line 68) provides a first-level enable gate not fully analyzed here, but reglk_ctrl asymmetry and the unconditional fuse_rdata_i assignment are directly evidenced in the source.

finding.summary: The pkt module exposes peripheral key locations (pkey_loc_o) from a fuse memory table without any authentication, privilege, or lifecycle guard — any requestor that asserts req_i can retrieve any key location by supplying an arbitrary fuse_indx_i.

evidence.description: The module accepts req_i (a single-bit enable) and fuse_indx_i (a 32-bit peripheral index) as plain inputs with no privilege level, authentication token, or lifecycle state input. There is no port for any access-control credential.
evidence.supports_claim: Any caller can assert req_i and supply an arbitrary index; there is no credential or privilege signal gating access.

evidence.description: The only guard present is the bounds check `fuse_indx_q < FUSE_MEM_SIZE` on line 154, which prevents out-of-range reads but does NOT restrict which requester may read which key location. No lock register, lifecycle check, debug-mode gate, or privilege comparison is present.
evidence.supports_claim: The sole guard is a range check, not an access-control check, leaving the entire fuse_indx_mem table readable by any agent that can drive req_i.

evidence.description: fuse_indx_mem is a ROM table holding AES key addresses (AES0Key0_0 through AES0Key0_2 etc.) indexed by peripheral ID. It is explicitly security-sensitive (key locations in ROM2).
evidence.supports_claim: AES key addresses stored in fuse_indx_mem are security-sensitive data that require access control.

evidence.description: On every posedge clk_i where req_i is asserted, fuse_indx_i (truncated to clog2(FUSE_MEM_SIZE) bits) is registered into fuse_indx_q and immediately used to index fuse_indx_mem, producing the key location on pkey_loc_o. No authentication, privilege, or lifecycle condition guards this path.
evidence.supports_claim: The complete absence of any access-control guard on the req_i → fuse_indx_q → pkey_loc_o path constitutes a missing permission check.

evidence.description: Because pkey_loc_o is a 64-bit output carrying the ROM2 address of a peripheral's AES key, an unprivileged requester that can assert req_i can enumerate all FUSE_MEM_SIZE (100) key locations by iterating fuse_indx_i, fully disclosing the AES key address map.
evidence.supports_claim: Full enumeration of AES key ROM addresses is possible without any privilege or authentication requirement.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt module is designed to look up and output the ROM2 address of a peripheral's AES key based on a caller-supplied index. The only implemented guard is an arithmetic bounds check (index < FUSE_MEM_SIZE). There is no privilege signal, no lifecycle/debug gate, no lock register, and no authentication input anywhere in the module. Any bus master that can assert the single-bit req_i can read the location of any key in the fuse table by sweeping fuse_indx_i from 0 to FUSE_MEM_SIZE-1.
finding.security_impact: Unauthorized disclosure of AES key ROM addresses. An unprivileged agent can enumerate all peripheral AES key locations, enabling targeted physical or logical attacks against those key storage locations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that access control is enforced at the interconnect level (e.g., a bus fabric that only allows privileged masters to reach this module's req_i port), but no such evidence exists within the provided RTL context. The module itself provides no internal access control.

finding.summary: In rng_wrapper, debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses all register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protections on sensitive RNG seed and polynomial registers, allowing reads and writes of cryptographic material regardless of the lock state.

evidence.description: Every read path in rng_wrapper uses the ternary pattern 'debug_mode_i ? <secret> : (reglk_ctrl_i[N] ? 0 : <secret>)'. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the reglk_ctrl_i lock bits are completely ignored and raw secret values (seed, poly128, poly64, poly32, poly16, seed128_big_o) are returned. The same pattern applies to writes (lines 127-141): debug_mode_i bypasses the lock and writes directly to the polynomial registers.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i acts as a master override that defeats all register-lock protection on cryptographic seed and polynomial state, creating an unauthorized readback and write path.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with acct_ctrl_i driven by acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][10] and debug_mode_i passed in as a top-level input, meaning any caller that can assert debug_mode_i gains full bypass of the RNG register locks.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal is a module-level input with no additional privilege check at the instantiation site, making the bypass reachable from the top level.

evidence.description: en is gated by 'en_acct && acct_ctrl_i' (access control), but the debug_mode_i bypass inside the always block operates independently of en gating — it only requires en to be true, not that debug_mode_i be restricted.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control via acct_ctrl_i gates bus enable but does not block the debug_mode_i-based lock bypass once a transaction is enabled.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper similarly reads sensitive fuse data (fuse_rdata_i, pkey_loc) gated only by reglk_ctrl_i bits, but unlike rng_wrapper there is no debug_mode_i bypass shown here — confirming the debug bypass is specific to rng_wrapper and represents an inconsistent protection model.
evidence.supports_claim: Cryptographic key material (pkey_loc, fuse data) is the class of sensitive resource being protected; rng_wrapper's debug bypass breaks equivalent protection for RNG secrets.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0014 provides direct source evidence that in rng_wrapper, every guarded read and write of seed and polynomial registers uses 'debug_mode_i ? <plain_value> : (reglk_ctrl_i[N] ? 0 : <plain_value>)'. When debug_mode_i=1, the reglk_ctrl_i lock is never evaluated, giving unrestricted access to all cryptographic RNG state. SNIP-0006 shows debug_mode_i is a plain input at instantiation with no additional privilege enforcement. SNIP-0013 confirms the en signal (access-controlled by acct_ctrl_i) is a prerequisite for entering the case statement, but within it, debug_mode_i overrides all locks. This constitutes a missing guard: the lock mechanism is rendered ineffective in debug mode.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged debug software that can assert debug_mode_i can read back or overwrite all RNG seed and polynomial registers (including seed128, poly128, poly64, poly32, poly16) regardless of the register-lock configuration set by system firmware. This leaks cryptographic keying material and allows manipulation of the RNG state, undermining the confidentiality and integrity of all downstream cryptographic operations using this RNG.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of debug_mode_i at the chip top level and the conditions under which it can be asserted in production are not visible in the provided snippets. If debug_mode_i is physically fused off in production devices, the practical exploitability is reduced, but the RTL-level vulnerability remains.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The source snippet shows only the port declarations of pkt_wrapper module, including AXI request/response ports, a register lock control input (reglk_ctrl_i), and an account control input (acct_ctrl_i). There is no source-code evidence of a missing guard, bypassed lock, unsafe reset behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update within the provided snippet. The single node and snippet do not contain any logic implementation that could demonstrate a permission-related vulnerability. Insufficient evidence exists in this chain context to support a finding.

finding.summary: The pkt_wrapper (fuse/key storage) register read path at address case 0 and 1 returns fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o without any reglk_ctrl_i lock check, while addresses 2-4 do apply lock bits. This inconsistency means software can unconditionally read fuse request and address state regardless of the register lock configuration.

evidence.description: Read case 0 returns `fuse_req_o` and case 1 returns `fuse_addr_o` with no reglk_ctrl_i guard, while cases 2, 3, and 4 gate their reads behind reglk_ctrl_i[4], reglk_ctrl_i[5], and reglk_ctrl_i[6] respectively. The missing guard on cases 0 and 1 allows any AXI requester to read fuse request and address state even when locks should prevent it.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent application of register-lock guards in the pkt_wrapper read mux permits unguarded readback of fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i[7:0] and acct_ctrl_i as protection inputs, indicating the design intent that register lock bits should guard access to fuse-related registers, yet these are not uniformly applied in the read path.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_ctrl_i is provided as a protection mechanism but is inconsistently applied within pkt_wrapper.

evidence.description: pkt_axi_req is wired from riscv_peripherals into pkt_wrapper.axi_req_i, meaning any software-initiated AXI transaction reaching this peripheral can trigger the unguarded register reads.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI requests from software reach pkt_wrapper without additional filtering beyond the wrapper's own (incomplete) lock checks.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface contains no privilege or lock checking; it purely translates AXI protocol signals to address/en/we outputs. All permission enforcement must occur in the calling wrapper, making the missing lock check in pkt_wrapper the only line of defense.
evidence.supports_claim: axi_lite_interface provides no access control; pkt_wrapper's reglk_ctrl_i checks are the sole guard, and they are absent for cases 0 and 1.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0006 shows a case statement that reads fuse_req_o (case 0) and fuse_addr_o (case 1) without consulting reglk_ctrl_i, while cases 2, 3, and 4 explicitly check reglk_ctrl_i bits before returning data. SNIP-0005 confirms that reglk_ctrl_i is a designed protection input. SNIP-0004 shows axi_lite_interface has no access control of its own, and SNIP-0003 shows the AXI request path is reachable from software. The combination means a software requester can read fuse address and fuse request status without the lock bits having any effect.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or unauthorized AXI master can read fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o from pkt_wrapper at any time regardless of the register lock state. These fields may leak sensitive fuse programming state or addresses that should be hidden once locks are asserted, enabling information disclosure of fuse provisioning data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o (whether they carry truly sensitive data vs. benign status) are not fully visible without the full pkt_wrapper implementation. However, the asymmetric guard pattern is clearly evidenced in the source snippet.

finding.summary: debug_mode_i bypasses register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection in rng_wrapper, allowing unrestricted readback of sensitive cryptographic seed and output registers via AXI response when debug mode is active.

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper's read-side logic, every sensitive register (seed[0..3], poly128_big, poly64_big, seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed16_big_o, rand_seg_o, cs_state_o) is returned in full when debug_mode_i is asserted, completely bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i lock bits. For example: 'rdata = debug_mode_i ? seed[3] : (reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? 0 : seed[3])'. This pattern repeats across all protected register offsets (lines 156-206).
evidence.supports_claim: When debug_mode_i is high, all register-lock protections enforced by reglk_ctrl_i are unconditionally bypassed, and full register contents are returned over the AXI read data channel.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper exposes rdata on its AXI response output (axi_resp_o), which is directly driven by the axi_lite_interface data_i port and ultimately reaches the AXI bus as read response data. The seed and polynomial registers are security-sensitive cryptographic state.
evidence.supports_claim: The rdata signal carrying unprotected sensitive values is wired to the AXI response output, making the disclosure exploitable by any AXI master that can trigger debug mode.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with debug_mode_i connected to the system-level debug_mode_i signal, acct_ctrl_i for access control, and reglk_ctrl_i for register locking. The debug bypass is therefore active whenever the system debug mode signal is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i input that bypasses all locks originates from the top-level system and is not itself gated by any privilege or lock check at the rng_wrapper boundary.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface unconditionally passes data_i to axi_resp_o.r.data (line 42: 'assign axi_resp_o.r.data = data_i'). There is no further filtering or masking of the read data within the AXI interface itself; whatever rdata the wrapper computes is directly exposed on the bus.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI interface has no secondary guard; the only protection opportunity is in the wrapper's rdata computation, which is bypassed by debug_mode_i.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper's read-side logic correctly gates all msg_out register reads exclusively through reglk_ctrl_i[3] without any debug_mode_i bypass (e.g., 'rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? 0 : msg_out[31:0]'). This confirms the design intent is that reglk_ctrl_i should protect registers, and the debug bypass in rng_wrapper is an inconsistency.
evidence.supports_claim: The RSA wrapper demonstrates that lock-only protection (no debug bypass) is the intended pattern, making the debug bypass in rng_wrapper an anomalous and unsafe deviation.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper read mux uses a ternary pattern 'debug_mode_i ? <sensitive_value> : (reglk_ctrl_i[N] ? 0 : <sensitive_value>)' for nearly every register offset. When debug_mode_i is asserted, reglk_ctrl_i lock bits are entirely ignored and raw cryptographic seeds, polynomials, and PRNG outputs are returned as AXI read data. The AXI interface passes this data directly to the bus without further filtering. The rsa_wrapper, by contrast, uses lock bits only with no debug bypass, showing the debug bypass in rng_wrapper deviates from the intended security model. Any bus master that can trigger debug mode (or any hardware/software path that sets debug_mode_i) can therefore read all protected RNG state regardless of lock configuration.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software that can activate debug mode can read all RNG seed material, polynomial configurations, and PRNG outputs through the AXI bus, completely defeating the register-lock access-control mechanism. This exposes the root secrets of the random number generator, enabling prediction of future outputs and potentially compromising cryptographic operations that depend on the RNG.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not known from the provided snippets how debug_mode_i is asserted at the system level (e.g., whether it requires a privileged JTAG operation or can be triggered by software). If debug_mode_i is strictly hardware-controlled and only accessible to trusted debug infrastructure, the practical exploitability is reduced, but the architectural violation (lock bypass) remains. The pkt_wrapper read-side logic (SNIP-0007) does not exhibit the debug bypass pattern, which is consistent.

finding.summary: The en_acct signal in pkt_wrapper (and analogous wrappers) is derived directly from the axi_lite_interface en_o output without being gated by the acct_ctrl_i access-control input. The axi_lite_interface drives en_o=1 purely based on AXI state-machine transitions (READ/WRITE states), with no knowledge of the acct_ctrl_i permission bit. The pkt_wrapper receives both en (from axi_lite_interface) and en_acct separately, but the guard that should AND en with acct_ctrl_i to form en_acct is absent or incomplete, meaning the peripheral register file can be accessed regardless of the privilege/access-control check.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface drives en_o=1 unconditionally when in READ state (line 103) or WRITE state (line 119), based solely on AXI handshake signals. There is no acct_ctrl input to this module; it has no awareness of access-control permissions. The en_o signal is wired to en_acct in the wrapper modules.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI lite interface asserts en_o (which becomes en_acct) without any privilege or access-control check, so the guard is absent at the point of access enablement.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper declares both 'en' and 'en_acct' as separate signals (line 42) and connects en_acct directly to axi_lite_interface's en_o (edge:connects:39). The acct_ctrl_i input (line 27) is received but there is no source evidence that en_acct is gated by acct_ctrl_i before being used to enable register access. The same axi_lite_interface instance drives both en and en_acct to its en_o output.
evidence.supports_claim: en_acct is directly the AXI interface enable with no AND of acct_ctrl_i, bypassing the access-control guard for PKT peripheral register access.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper similarly declares en and en_acct (line 57) and connects en_acct to axi_lite_interface en_o (edge:connects:76). acct_ctrl_i is an input (line 20 in SNIP-0010) but no gating with en_acct is shown in the source snippets.
evidence.supports_claim: Same pattern: en_acct is driven from unauthenticated AXI enable in rng_wrapper, bypassing acct_ctrl_i check.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper declares en and en_acct (line 37) and connects en_acct to axi_lite_interface en_o (edge:connects:104). acct_ctrl_i is an input (line 23) but no source evidence of gating with en_acct.
evidence.supports_claim: Same pattern: en_acct is driven from unauthenticated AXI enable in rsa_wrapper, bypassing acct_ctrl_i check.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is the privilege-level-indexed access control signal derived from acc_ctrl, computed as acc_ctrl[j*4+i] OR'd with a special case for peripheral 5. This is the intended guard that should gate access to peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral_index] is the correct access-control value that should gate en_acct but is not ANDed with the AXI en_o in the wrapper modules.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper instantiation passes acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][13], demonstrating intent for privilege-gated access. However, inside rsa_wrapper the en_acct is driven directly from axi_lite_interface en_o without using acct_ctrl_i as a gate.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-control input is connected at instantiation but not enforced inside the wrapper, confirming the bypass.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper handles fuse memory access (fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, fuse_rdata_i) — a sensitive resource containing cryptographic key material (pkey_loc). Unguarded en_acct means any AXI master can trigger reads/writes to this sensitive resource.
evidence.supports_claim: The protected resource (fuse/PKT key storage) is accessible without enforcing the access-control permission check.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design pattern across pkt_wrapper, rng_wrapper, and rsa_wrapper is: (1) receive acct_ctrl_i from the privilege-level-indexed access control matrix, (2) instantiate axi_lite_interface which produces en_o based purely on AXI handshake state, (3) wire axi_lite_interface en_o to both 'en' and 'en_acct'. The separation into en vs en_acct suggests the intent was that en_acct = en & acct_ctrl_i, but no such AND gate is present in the source snippets. The axi_lite_interface has no acct_ctrl port at all. Therefore any AXI requester can assert aw_valid or ar_valid to cause en_acct=1, bypassing the privilege check embedded in acct_ctrl_i.
finding.security_impact: Any privilege level that can send AXI transactions to the PKT, RSA, or RNG peripheral address range can read or write the peripheral registers — including sensitive fuse/key storage in PKT — regardless of the access-control matrix configured in acc_ctrl. This nullifies the hardware access-control enforcement for these security-critical peripherals.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal register-file logic of pkt_wrapper beyond line 56 is not shown in snippets; it is possible that en_acct is further gated by acct_ctrl_i inside the register file logic not captured here. However, the naming convention (en vs en_acct both wired to the same axi_lite en_o) and absence of any AND with acct_ctrl_i in shown code strongly suggests the guard is missing.

finding.summary: fuse_addr_o (the current fuse memory address) is readable without any register-lock or access-control guard in pkt_wrapper, exposing internal fuse memory addressing to unprivileged software.

evidence.description: In the register-read case statement at address offset 1 (line 101), rdata is assigned directly to fuse_addr_o with no reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i check. Adjacent cases at offsets 2, 3, and 4 each gate their return value behind reglk_ctrl_i bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[4], [5], [6]), but offset 1 has no such protection.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_addr_o can be read back by any agent that can issue an AXI read to the PKT peripheral at address offset 1, bypassing all register-lock protection.

evidence.description: fuse_addr_o is declared as an output port of pkt_wrapper and carries the 32-bit address used to index the fuse memory. The fuse memory holds cryptographic keys and other secure data (as shown in SNIP-0011).
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_addr_o references the address space of a sensitive resource (fuse memory holding key material), making its unguarded readback security-relevant.

evidence.description: fuse_mem contains constant key values (JTAG hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash) and is described as holding 'all the secure data'. The addr_i it receives comes from pkt_wrapper.fuse_addr_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse memory is a sensitive resource; leaking its current access address reveals which key/data slot is being accessed.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i = reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8] and acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][5], indicating lock and privilege-level controls exist for this peripheral. However these are not applied to offset-1 readback of fuse_addr_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard infrastructure is present at the top level but is not applied to the fuse_addr_o readback path inside pkt_wrapper.

evidence.description: Cases 2, 3, and 4 explicitly check reglk_ctrl_i[4], reglk_ctrl_i[5], and reglk_ctrl_i[6] before returning sensitive data, but case 1 (fuse_addr_o) has no analogous check, confirming the missing guard is not a design intent omission for the whole block.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent application of reglk_ctrl_i guards within the same case statement demonstrates that the guard for case 1 was unintentionally omitted.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper module exposes a register-mapped read interface. At address offset 1, it returns fuse_addr_o (the active fuse memory address) without any reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i check. Every other sensitive offset in the same case statement is protected by a reglk_ctrl_i bit. The fuse memory holds cryptographic key material, and knowing which address is currently being accessed can assist attacks. The guard infrastructure (reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i) is wired in from the parent module but simply not applied for this specific field.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software agent that can read the PKT peripheral at AXI offset 1 can determine the current fuse memory address being targeted by the PKT engine. This leaks internal state about which cryptographic key or data slot is in use, potentially aiding side-channel or replay attacks against fuse-stored key material.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to access the PKT AXI region is not shown in the available snippets; if the peripheral is already fully restricted to privileged software the practical impact is reduced. Additionally, it is unclear whether fuse_addr_o can be controlled externally or only reflects internally-driven addresses.

finding.summary: In pkt_wrapper, fuse memory read data (fuse_rdata_i) is exposed via AXI register reads without a register-lock guard on two code paths, and the default read case exposes fuse content unconditionally when en is asserted. Specifically, address case 4 uses reglk_ctrl_i[6] to gate fuse_rdata_i, but the default case (lines 108-110) assigns rdata = 32'b0 only based on an address range check with no reglk guard, and the fallthrough at line 96 (rdata = fuse_rdata_i before the case statement) exposes fuse data for any enabled access before the case overrides it, meaning some address slots leak raw fuse data. Additionally, the fuse_mem itself has no access control: any requester that drives req_i and addr_i receives the sensitive fuse contents directly with no privilege or lock check.

evidence.description: At line 96, rdata is unconditionally assigned fuse_rdata_i whenever en is asserted, before the case statement. For any address that is not matched by cases 0-4, the default case (lines 108-110) only zeroes rdata when fuse_addr_o <= 110, but for addresses above 110 the earlier assignment of rdata = fuse_rdata_i persists. Additionally, case 4 (line 107) guards fuse_rdata_i with reglk_ctrl_i[6], but there is no equivalent guard for the fallthrough path at line 96 or the default branch.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive fuse read data is exposed on AXI bus without complete register-lock protection across all address cases.

evidence.description: fuse_mem stores secure data including JTAG hash and key material. Its rdata_o output is directly driven to any requester supplying req_i and addr_i with no access control logic inside the module.
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse memory is a sensitive resource containing cryptographic keys with no internal access guard.

evidence.description: fuse_mem line 135 assigns rdata_o = mem[addr_q] with only a bounds check. There is no privilege check, no register-lock input, and no enable gating on the output. Any req_i assertion with a valid address yields the fuse content.
evidence.supports_claim: Fuse memory output has no access control: missing lock/privilege guard on rdata_o.

evidence.description: The fuse_mem is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with req_i driven by pkt_wrapper's fuse_req_o and addr_i driven by fuse_addr_o. The pkt_wrapper can freely assert fuse_req_o and set fuse_addr_o via AXI writes (lines 83-85 of pkt_wrapper), and the returned fuse_rdata is fed back to pkt_wrapper.fuse_rdata_i without additional filtering.
evidence.supports_claim: An AXI-accessible peripheral can freely request any fuse memory address and retrieve the data.

evidence.description: Lines 103 and 105 gate pkey_loc readback with reglk_ctrl_i[4] and reglk_ctrl_i[5]. Line 107 gates fuse_rdata_i with reglk_ctrl_i[6]. However, the assignment at line 96 (rdata = fuse_rdata_i) executes before the case and is not guarded by any reglk bit, creating a bypass for addresses not matched by the locked cases.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent register-lock application leaves fuse_rdata_i readable through unguarded code paths.

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper, debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses reglk_ctrl_i for both reads and writes of seed and polynomial values (e.g., line 127: debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? ...)). This means asserting debug_mode_i circumvents all register lock protections.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode bypass of register locks in rng_wrapper is a systemic pattern showing incomplete protection enforcement in this security chain.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper reads fuse memory contents and exposes them over AXI. The read logic assigns rdata = fuse_rdata_i unconditionally when en is asserted (line 96) before a case statement that selectively applies reglk guards only for specific address offsets (cases 2, 3, 4). For any address not matching cases 0-4 where fuse_addr_o > 110, the raw fuse data leaks through without any lock check. The fuse_mem itself contains no internal access control. Together, these create a path from AXI read requests to sensitive fuse memory contents that bypasses register lock protection for a range of addresses.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI bus master can read sensitive cryptographic key material (JTAG hash, ikey, okey from fuse_mem) by writing appropriate fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o values and then reading back fuse_rdata_i through pkt_wrapper AXI registers at addresses not covered by the reglk gate, exfiltrating secret key material stored in the fuse memory.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact address decode ranges and which addresses fall through the unguarded default path require runtime knowledge of FUSE_MEM_SIZE and the address mapping. The acct_ctrl_i gating of en (line 68) provides a privilege filter but its correct configuration is not fully verifiable from snippets alone. The full pkt_wrapper read logic context beyond the shown lines may have additional guards not captured here.

finding.summary: The fuse_mem module exposes sensitive cryptographic key hashes (jtag_hash_o, okey_hash_o, ikey_hash_o) as hardcoded constants directly readable from combinational outputs with no access control, no lock guard, and no privilege check. Additionally, the fuse_mem rdata_o path allows arbitrary AXI-addressable readback of the fuse memory contents driven by pkt_wrapper's fuse_req_o without any reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl gating on the fuse_mem interface itself.

evidence.description: fuse_mem contains hardcoded 256-bit JTAG, okey, and ikey hashes in a const ROM array. Lines 137-139 show these are directly and unconditionally assigned to output ports jtag_hash_o, okey_hash_o, ikey_hash_o without any enable, lock, or privilege gate.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive cryptographic material is permanently driven on output wires with no access control.

evidence.description: The fuse_mem module interface (lines 7-12) exposes jtag_hash_o, ikey_hash_o, okey_hash_o as plain outputs and rdata_o as a readable output. There is no reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, or any permission input port on fuse_mem itself — the module has no mechanism to block read access to its secure contents.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_mem lacks any permission or lock input, so its secure hash outputs are always readable by any connected consumer.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates fuse_mem and connects fuse_req from pkt_wrapper directly to fuse_mem.req_i, and fuse_mem.rdata_o back to pkt_wrapper via fuse_rdata. No access control signals are passed to fuse_mem at the instantiation site.
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse memory read path is reachable from pkt_wrapper's fuse_req_o without any permission enforcement at the fuse_mem boundary.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i but these are used only to gate the AXI-facing en_acct signal for the peripheral register interface. The fuse_req_o output (line 30) is a separate path whose gating by reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i is not evident from the port declarations or internal signal definitions shown.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control inputs present in pkt_wrapper do not demonstrably gate the fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o outputs, leaving the fuse read path potentially ungated.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals wires reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8] and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][5] to pkt_wrapper, but the fuse_mem instantiation at lines 1699-1708 receives no reglk or acc_ctrl signals — only fuse_req and fuse_addr from pkt_wrapper are forwarded, confirming the fuse_mem itself has no permission state.
evidence.supports_claim: Permission enforcement is attempted only at the pkt_wrapper boundary but fuse_mem itself has no guard, so if pkt_wrapper's guard is incomplete, secrets are exposed.

evidence.description: jtag_hash, okey_hash, and ikey_hash are 256-bit logic signals in riscv_peripherals that are directly driven from fuse_mem outputs. These represent JTAG authentication hashes and key material used for privilege escalation decisions (jtag_unlock).
evidence.supports_claim: Unguarded readback of these hash values enables an attacker to retrieve the expected JTAG authentication secret, facilitating privilege escalation or debug bypass.

finding.reasoning_summary: The fuse_mem module stores and exposes JTAG and key hashes as hardcoded combinational outputs with no access-control port whatsoever. Any module connected to fuse_mem's outputs receives the full 256-bit secrets unconditionally. The rdata_o path allows AXI-addressable readback of the fuse ROM contents gated only by req_i, which is driven by pkt_wrapper's fuse_req_o. While pkt_wrapper has reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs, the fuse_mem instantiation in riscv_peripherals receives none of these signals, and the fuse_mem module itself has no lock or privilege input. This creates a missing guard vulnerability on a sensitive cryptographic resource.
finding.security_impact: An attacker able to issue AXI transactions to the pkt_wrapper peripheral, or able to read the jtag_hash/okey_hash/ikey_hash wires (e.g., via another peripheral with memory-mapped access or debug probe), can recover the JTAG authentication hash values stored in fuse_mem. These hashes are used for jtag_unlock decisions, enabling authentication bypass and full debug/privilege escalation on the SoC.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of pkt_wrapper is not shown beyond port declarations and AXI interface instantiation; it is possible that fuse_req_o is internally gated by reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i in logic not present in the provided snippets. However, fuse_mem's own interface definitively lacks any permission input, meaning the jtag_hash_o/okey_hash_o/ikey_hash_o outputs are always driven regardless of any upstream gating.

finding.summary: In pkt_wrapper, the pkey_loc (private/public key location) register is readable via the AXI bus, protected only by reglk_ctrl_i[4] and reglk_ctrl_i[5]. However, unlike rng_wrapper and rsa_wrapper which also check debug_mode_i to unconditionally allow access, there is no debug_mode_i bypass for pkey_loc readback—this is a minor asymmetry. More critically, address cases 0 and 1 (fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o) are returned with no reglk_ctrl_i guard at all, and address case 4 (fuse_rdata_i raw readback) is protected only by reglk_ctrl_i[6] with no debug_mode_i override. Additionally, when reglk_ctrl_i bits are 0 (unlocked), the pkey_loc upper and lower 32-bit halves are directly returned to the bus caller without any privilege/access-control check beyond the 'en' gating (which itself is gated by acct_ctrl_i). The fuse_addr_o register (case 1) is returned unconditionally with no lock bit protection whatsoever.

evidence.description: In the read-side always block of pkt_wrapper, address case 1 returns fuse_addr_o directly as rdata with no reglk_ctrl_i lock bit check and no debug_mode_i guard: 'rdata = fuse_addr_o;' This means the fuse memory address register is always readable by any enabled accessor.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_addr_o is returned with zero lock/privilege protection, unlike other sensitive registers in the same module.

evidence.description: Address case 0 returns fuse_req_o ('{31'b0, fuse_req_o}') with no reglk_ctrl_i guard bit. Any enabled bus master can read the fuse request state.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_req_o has no register lock protection in the read path.

evidence.description: Cases 2 and 3 guard pkey_loc reads with reglk_ctrl_i[4] and reglk_ctrl_i[5] respectively. However, unlike rng_wrapper (SNIP-0015), there is no debug_mode_i override path for pkey_loc in pkt_wrapper, creating an inconsistency but not a direct bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: pkey_loc is conditionally protected by reglk_ctrl_i bits, but cases 0 and 1 in the same module are unprotected.

evidence.description: The 'en' signal is gated by 'en_acct && acct_ctrl_i' (line 68), providing access-control gating. However, this is the only guard for cases 0 and 1; there is no additional reglk_ctrl_i protection for those address cases.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_ctrl_i is the sole access guard for fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o readback, with no register-lock protection.

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper, debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses reglk_ctrl_i for all sensitive register reads (seeds, poly values, etc.), e.g. 'rdata = debug_mode_i ? seed[3] : (reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? 0 : seed[3])'. This debug bypass allows full register readback when debug mode is active, regardless of lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i provides an unconditional bypass of all reglk_ctrl_i protections in rng_wrapper, which is a privilege-escalation vector via debug mode.

evidence.description: fuse_mem stores cryptographic key material including JTAG hash, ikey_hash, and okey_hash. The fuse_addr_o (unprotected in case 1 of pkt_wrapper read path) controls which fuse memory location is read, and fuse_rdata_i (case 4, protected by reglk_ctrl_i[6]) returns the raw fuse memory data.
evidence.supports_claim: Fuse memory contains sensitive cryptographic key material, making unprotected readback of fuse_addr_o and conditionally protected readback of fuse_rdata_i security-relevant.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper read path exposes fuse_req_o (case 0) and fuse_addr_o (case 1) with no reglk_ctrl_i lock bit protection—only the acct_ctrl_i-gated 'en' signal guards these. The fuse memory contains cryptographic key material. Additionally, rng_wrapper's debug_mode_i bypass unconditionally overrides all reglk_ctrl_i protections for sensitive seed and polynomial register reads, which is a systemic debug-mode privilege escalation vulnerability. These together constitute concrete missing-guard and debug-bypass vulnerabilities.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access and acct_ctrl_i=1 (or debug_mode_i=1 for rng_wrapper) can read fuse memory addresses, fuse request state, and (in rng_wrapper) all PRNG seed and polynomial values regardless of register lock state. This can leak cryptographic key material and PRNG internal state, undermining the security of the PKT and RNG subsystems.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact bit assignments of reglk_ctrl_i and the conditions under which acct_ctrl_i and debug_mode_i are asserted at the system level are not fully visible in the provided snippets. The pkt_wrapper does not use debug_mode_i at all, so the absence of a debug bypass there may be intentional.

finding.summary: The fuse_mem module, which stores sensitive key material (JTAG hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash), lacks any access-control or register-lock guard on its read interface. The pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs, but the fuse_mem itself is directly readable by anyone who can drive fuse_req and fuse_addr through pkt_wrapper's fuse interface, with no visible enforcement that reglk_ctrl_i actually gates reads from fuse_mem.

evidence.description: fuse_mem stores sensitive key values (JTAG hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash) in a const logic array and exposes them via req_i/addr_i/rdata_o with no access-control input ports on the module itself.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_mem has no permission or lock ports; all access control must be enforced externally.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i (8-bit register lock) and acct_ctrl_i but also directly drives fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o to the fuse_mem. The module exposes en and en_acct as separate signals, suggesting reglk may only gate the AXI register interface and not the fuse read path.
evidence.supports_claim: The fuse access path (fuse_req_o / fuse_addr_o) originates inside pkt_wrapper independently of the AXI register-lock gating.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates pkt_wrapper with reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8] and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][5], then wires pkt_wrapper's fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o directly to the fuse_mem with no intervening lock check between the wrapper and fuse_mem.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard is interposed between pkt_wrapper's fuse output signals and fuse_mem at the riscv_peripherals level.

evidence.description: fuse_mem is instantiated with req_i=fuse_req and addr_i=fuse_addr driven directly from pkt_wrapper outputs. There is no conditional enable or lock check visible here that would block reads when reglk_ctrl_i is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_mem reads are not gated by reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl at the point of instantiation.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl is a 112-bit vector and acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl; these are the permission state signals distributed to peripherals including pkt_wrapper, but not to fuse_mem itself.
evidence.supports_claim: Permission state exists in the system but is not propagated into the fuse_mem module.

finding.reasoning_summary: The fuse_mem module holds sensitive cryptographic key material and exposes a simple req/addr/rdata interface with no access-control ports of its own. In riscv_peripherals, pkt_wrapper is granted reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl inputs, but the pkt_wrapper's fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o outputs are wired directly to fuse_mem without any intervening gate controlled by the lock/access signals. The fuse_mem module itself accepts no permission or lock inputs. This means that once pkt_wrapper issues a fuse read request (regardless of whether the AXI register interface is locked), fuse_mem will respond with its sensitive data. evidence.description: The dma_axi_req structure carries AXI4 bus requests from the DMA engine to potentially memory-mapped resources. The aw.lock='0 and ar.lock='0 assignments (lines 1961, 1972) also disable atomic/exclusive access signaling, while aw.cache='0 and ar.cache='0 (lines 1962, 1973) disable memory type signaling. Together with prot='0, the DMA has no mechanism to signal unprivileged or non-secure transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: All DMA AXI transactions are permanently presented to the interconnect as privileged secure accesses, which may allow the DMA to access memory regions that should be restricted to privileged or secure masters only.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet at lines 1957-1976 shows a block of tie-off assignments for AXI signals not used by the AXI-lite conversion layer. Critically, dma_axi_req.aw.prot and dma_axi_req.ar.prot are both hardcoded to '0. In AXI, PROT[1]=0 means Secure access and PROT[0]=0 means Privileged access. This means every DMA transaction on both write (AW) and read (AR) channels is permanently marked as a privileged secure transaction. If any downstream AXI slave or interconnect uses PROT-based filtering (e.g., a TrustZone-aware memory controller or peripheral protection unit), the DMA will always appear as a trusted privileged master, bypassing any non-secure or unprivileged access restrictions. This is a missing guard/incorrect protection state: the prot field should reflect the actual privilege and security context of the DMA initiator rather than a fixed value of zero.
finding.security_impact: All DMA-initiated memory transactions bypass AXI protection checks enforced by downstream slaves or interconnect components. A compromised or malicious DMA configuration could access secure memory regions or privileged peripherals that should be inaccessible to non-secure/unprivileged initiators, potentially leading to privilege escalation or confidential data exfiltration via the DMA engine.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual security impact depends on whether any downstream AXI slaves or interconnect components enforce PROT-based access control. If all downstream components ignore the PROT field, this is a latent issue rather than an active vulnerability. The chain context does not include evidence of downstream protection enforcement logic. Additionally, the comment 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite' suggests this may be an intentional simplification for an AXI-lite bridge context, but the security consequence of hardcoding prot='0 remains regardless of intent.

finding.summary: The fuse memory (fuse_mem) exposes sensitive key material via the AXI-mapped rdata_o output without any register-lock (reglk_ctrl) or access-control (acct_ctrl) guard. The pkt_wrapper read path at address offset 4 (case 4) does gate fuse_rdata_i behind reglk_ctrl_i[6], but address offsets 0 and 1 return fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o unconditionally, and the default branch also returns fuse data (rdata = fuse_rdata_i) before the case statement overrides it—meaning an attacker who controls the address can read raw fuse memory contents with no privilege check.

The module has no write-enable, no lock port, and no privilege input—it exposes all contents to whoever drives req_i and addr_i.

No req_i qualification, no reglk check, no privilege level check, and no reset-time suppression is applied to these outputs. While the AXI-mapped rdata_o path is gated by req_i and pkt_wrapper applies en/reglk guards to its register reads, the hash outputs entirely bypass all such controls. They are wired directly in riscv_peripherals and available continuously to any connected logic.

This breaks the confidentiality guarantee of fuse-based key storage and can enable JTAG unlock, key derivation attacks, or authentication bypass.

However, the source evidence clearly shows the outputs are driven unconditionally from fuse_mem with no protection, which is itself the vulnerability regardless of downstream handling.

finding.summary: AXI protection (prot) field for HMAC peripheral is hardwired to zero, disabling privilege, security, and instruction/data access type signaling on both write and read channels.

evidence.description: Line 1591 assigns hmac_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1602 assigns hmac_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The AXI4 AxPROT field encodes three access-type bits: privileged/unprivileged (bit 0), secure/non-secure (bit 1), and instruction/data (bit 2). Hardwiring both the AW and AR PROT fields to zero means every transaction to the HMAC peripheral is unconditionally marked as unprivileged, secure-world, and data access, regardless of the actual initiator privilege level. Downstream bus interconnects or security monitors that rely on AxPROT to enforce access-control policies will therefore see no useful protection information and cannot distinguish between a privileged kernel request and an unprivileged user-mode request.
evidence.supports_claim: hmac_axi_req.aw.prot and hmac_axi_req.ar.prot are permanently forced to 0, stripping all AXI privilege and security signaling from HMAC bus transactions.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet at lines 1591 and 1602 explicitly ties the AXI AxPROT signals for the HMAC peripheral to all-zeros. These fields are the standard AXI4 mechanism for communicating transaction privilege level and security domain to interconnect components and peripheral-side access checkers. Forcing them to zero means: (1) any downstream firewall or TrustZone-aware interconnect that checks AxPROT[1] (Non-Secure bit) may grant access as if the transaction originates from a secure master, which can allow non-secure software to read or write HMAC key material and digests; (2) privilege-checking logic that inspects AxPROT[0] will always see 'unprivileged', potentially blocking legitimate privileged accesses or, conversely, granting all accesses if the checker expects unprivileged=0. The HMAC peripheral holds cryptographically sensitive data (keys, intermediate digests), making incorrect protection signaling a meaningful security risk.
finding.security_impact: Software running at any privilege level will have its HMAC transactions presented to the bus fabric and peripheral as unprivileged, non-secure, data accesses. Security monitors or TrustZone-aware interconnects that gate HMAC register access based on AxPROT will either incorrectly allow non-secure/unprivileged access to HMAC key and digest registers, or incorrectly block privileged secure accesses. In systems with bus-level firewalls this can expose HMAC secrets to unauthorized software.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual impact depends on whether the downstream interconnect or HMAC wrapper performs AxPROT-based access filtering. If no such filtering exists the hardwired zero is a latent risk rather than an immediately exploitable bypass. The snippet does not show the interconnect configuration or any firewall policy, so the severity is conditional on the system integration.

finding.reasoning_summary: The fuse_mem module permanently drives all three 256-bit secret key hashes (jtag_hash_o, okey_hash_o, ikey_hash_o) via unconditional continuous assign statements (lines 137-139). Unlike rdata_o which has a bounds guard, these hash outputs have zero gating. The req_i signal only controls addr_q registration and rdata_o, not the hash outputs. finding.summary: The pkt_wrapper (and similarly rng_wrapper and rsa_wrapper) receives AXI requests that are passed directly into axi_lite_interface without any privilege/permission guard within the axi_lite_interface itself. The pkt_wrapper accepts reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs for access control, but the axi_lite_interface it instantiates has no such inputs and performs no access-control checks — it unconditionally processes any AXI request presented to it. The AXI protection field (aw.prot / ar.prot) is hardwired to zero in riscv_peripherals, meaning no privilege level is conveyed to the peripheral, and the axi_lite_interface never checks any permission before granting read/write access to the underlying register file.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design declares per-peripheral access-control signals (reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i) at the wrapper module boundaries for PKT, RSA, and RNG peripherals. However, the shared axi_lite_interface module that actually services AXI read/write transactions has no ports or logic for these signals and never checks any privilege or lock condition before asserting en_o/we_o and performing the underlying register access. Compounding this, the AXI AxPROT fields — the standard AXI mechanism for conveying privilege and security state — are hardwired to zero before being sent to these peripherals, so even a downstream implementation that checked prot would receive no useful information. The result is that any AXI master that can reach these peripherals can unconditionally read and write their registers regardless of privilege level or register-lock state.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure AXI master can read sensitive data (RNG seeds, PKT/fuse key material) and write to security-critical registers (RSA key/operation registers, PKT lock state) without any hardware-enforced permission check, bypassing the declared reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl protection model.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal logic within each wrapper that uses reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i after the AXI interface (e.g., per-register read masking or write blocking) is not shown in the provided snippets. It is possible some protection is applied at the register-file level downstream of en_o/we_o, but the AXI-layer itself is confirmed to have no guard, and AxPROT is confirmed zeroed.

evidence.description: The PLIC AXI state machine translates reg-interface requests from the CPU into AXI transactions on plic_master. finding.reasoning_summary: Across all five security-sensitive peripherals (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0), the AXI AxPROT[2:0] fields are unconditionally tied to '0 via continuous assignments. In the AXI4 specification, AxPROT encodes three bits: privileged vs. unprivileged, secure vs. non-secure, and instruction vs. data. Forcing all three to zero means every transaction is tagged as unprivileged, non-secure, and data. Any interconnect firewall or peripheral guard that relies on PROT to enforce privilege separation or TrustZone-style secure/non-secure partitioning will see all accesses as identical unprivileged non-secure data reads/writes. This effectively removes the protection signaling layer for critical peripherals including the cryptographic accelerators (RSA, AES0) and the RNG.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure bus master that can inject transactions on these AXI interfaces will have the same PROT encoding as a privileged secure master, defeating interconnect-level access control. For cryptographic peripherals (RSA, AES0, RNG), this means key material and random outputs may be accessible without privilege enforcement. For the PLIC, interrupt routing registers are accessible as if from an unprivileged non-secure context. Any downstream MPU, firewall, or TrustZone-aware slave that enforces PROT-based access control is rendered ineffective.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream interconnect firewall configuration and whether any PROT-based enforcement is present at the slave side are not visible in this chain. If no interconnect firewall checks PROT, the practical exploitability depends on whether other access controls (address decode, privilege level checks in wrappers) exist. The rsa_wrapper instantiation shows reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl inputs suggesting some wrapper-level access control exists, but the PROT zeroing still removes the AXI-layer protection signal.

finding.summary: The rng_wrapper module exposes sensitive RNG internal state (seeds, polynomial taps, random segments, CS state) as output ports explicitly labeled 'for debug mode', and the rng_top instantiation reset line is driven by `rst_ni && ~rst_10`, where rst_10 is an externally controllable reset signal that can reset the LFSR state independently of the system reset, potentially allowing an attacker to force known LFSR state. Additionally, the reglk_ctrl lock signal is passed to rng_wrapper but source evidence does not show the lock bits actually gating write access to the seed/poly registers inside rng_wrapper, leaving the question of whether the register lock is enforced on sensitive RNG configuration registers.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper declares outputs seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed16_big_o, rand_seg_o, and cs_state_o explicitly marked as 'signals for debug mode', exposing the full internal LFSR seed and state to outside the module.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive LFSR seed and state values are exposed as named debug outputs without any guard conditioned on a privilege level or lock signal.

evidence.description: rng_top is instantiated with reset `rst_n(rst_ni && ~rst_10)`, meaning asserting the external rst_10 signal forces a reset of all LFSR entropy sources (rng_128, rng_64, rng_32, rng_16) to their initial seed state, regardless of normal system operation. This allows an attacker with write access to the rst_10 control path to repeatedly reset the RNG to a known state.
evidence.supports_claim: An externally controllable reset (rst_10) can force the RNG subsystem into a predictable initial state, destroying entropy.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl[10*8+7:10*8] as the lock control and acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][10] as access control. The inline comment 'why write enable flag?' suggests uncertainty about whether the lock bits are correctly applied.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl field passed to rng_wrapper may not correctly protect all RNG registers, as indicated by the developer comment questioning its purpose.

evidence.description: rng_top exposes seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o, rand_seg128_o, rand_seg64_o, rand_seg32_o, rand_seg16_o, and cs_state_o as output ports, propagating the complete LFSR internal state upward through the hierarchy.
evidence.supports_claim: All LFSR seed and segment values are directly observable outputs of rng_top with no access restriction at the hardware level.

evidence.description: All four LFSR submodules (rng_128, rng_64, rng_32, rng_16) receive the same rst_n line from rng_top, which is driven by `rst_ni && ~rst_10`. Asserting rst_10 resets all entropy sources simultaneously.
evidence.supports_claim: External rst_10 can force all LFSR stages to reset simultaneously, collapsing entropy to initial seed values.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain shows that the RNG subsystem (rng_wrapper/rng_top) exposes its internal LFSR seeds and state via debug output ports that are wired up through the hierarchy without any privilege or lock gating on the readback path. The rng_top reset line incorporates an external rst_10 signal that can independently reset all four LFSR entropy sources to known initial states. The developer comment in riscv_peripherals.sv ('why write enable flag?') indicates the reglk_ctrl integration may be incorrect. Together these form a concrete permission-security vulnerability: sensitive RNG state is readable via debug outputs and the entropy can be reset to known state via an external control signal.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert rst_10 can repeatedly reset the RNG to a known seed state, making the random number output predictable. The debug output ports (seed*_big_o, rand_seg_o, cs_state_o) expose the complete internal LFSR state, allowing full state reconstruction and future output prediction. This breaks the security of any cryptographic operation depending on the RNG (e.g., RSA key generation seeded from rng_wrapper).
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not include the full rng_wrapper register-access logic that would confirm whether reglk_ctrl bits actually gate writes to the seed/poly registers. It is also unclear which privilege levels or software interfaces control rst_10, and whether the debug outputs (seed*_big_o etc.) are actually connected to software-readable registers at the riscv_peripherals level. The fuse_mem snippet shows hardcoded key constants but their relationship to RNG seeding is not shown in the provided snippets.

Any transaction presented on axi_req_i will be completed.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI interface provides no permission enforcement, so the reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl provided to the wrapper are not effective at blocking AXI transactions to the RNG peripheral.

evidence.description: The same pattern applies to rsa_wrapper (also has reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, debug_mode_i) with axi_req_i.ar.prot similarly tied to zero, showing this is a systemic issue across security-sensitive peripherals.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper and other security-sensitive peripherals are instantiated with register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i), access-control (acct_ctrl_i), and debug-mode (debug_mode_i) inputs suggesting access should be gated.
