The axi_lite_interface itself has no privilege, lock, or debug-mode check in its state machine, so any requester can read or write the RNG registers regardless of privilege level or lock state.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious software agent could read RNG seed/output registers or write to RNG configuration registers without being blocked by the register-lock or access-control mechanisms. This could allow entropy harvesting, seed manipulation, or denial of randomness to privileged software, undermining cryptographic security on the SoC.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets for rng_wrapper do not show the complete internal logic after the axi_lite_interface instantiation, so it is possible that en_o/we_o outputs from axi_lite_interface are further gated by reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i before reaching registers. Forcing all three bits to zero means every SHA-256 bus transaction appears as an unprivileged, non-secure data access regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating software.
evidence.supports_claim: The AxPROT fields on the SHA-256 AXI interface are hardwired to zero, bypassing the AXI privilege and security signaling that downstream bus monitors, firewalls, or memory controllers may rely on to enforce access control.

evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard, mux, or privilege-forwarding logic that could conditionally set AxPROT based on the requestor privilege level, confirming the missing protection.

evidence.description: The SHA-256 cryptographic accelerator is a security-sensitive peripheral. finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that sha256_axi_req.aw.prot and sha256_axi_req.ar.prot are unconditionally tied to '0 with no input from the actual privilege level or security state of the requesting agent. In an AXI4 system, AxPROT[1] (Secure/Non-Secure) and AxPROT[0] (Privileged/Unprivileged) are relied upon by TrustZone-aware and firewall-protected interconnects. Hardwiring these to zero on the SHA-256 cryptographic peripheral path means all bus-level security and privilege enforcement that depends on AxPROT signaling is effectively bypassed or misconfigured.
finding.security_impact: Any bus-level firewall or memory protection unit that uses AxPROT to gate access to the SHA-256 accelerator or to memory regions it can reach will receive incorrect privilege/security information. This could allow unprivileged or non-secure software to drive SHA-256 transactions that appear as unprivileged/non-secure to the interconnect, potentially bypassing TrustZone-based isolation, or could cause legitimate privileged accesses to be rejected if the firewall requires secure/privileged AxPROT signaling. The cryptographic integrity and isolation guarantees of the SHA-256 peripheral are weakened.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual interconnect or firewall policy that consumes AxPROT is not visible in the provided snippets, so it is uncertain whether any downstream component enforces policy based on AxPROT. evidence.description: rng_top declares output ports seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o, rand_seg128_o, rand_seg64_o, rand_seg32_o, rand_seg16_o, and cs_state_o that expose the internal LFSR seed and intermediate random segments.
evidence.supports_claim: Internal RNG seed state is routed to output ports with no in-module protection.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper declares seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed16_big_o and rand_seg_o signals described as 'for debug mode', and seed16_big_o is connected directly from rng_top's seed16_o output (edge:connects:93). The comment 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode' indicates these are intended debug readback paths.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive seed values are specifically plumbed as debug-readable outputs.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper instantiates rng_top with .rst_n(rst_ni && ~rst_10) and connects seed16_big_o from rng_top's seed16_o. The read-data mux in rng_wrapper (lines 207-210) returns rdata for various address cases. There is no shown guard conditioned on debug_mode_i or acct_ctrl_i before seed values are placed into rdata, meaning any AXI read to the appropriate address can retrieve the seed.
evidence.supports_claim: Seed readback path lacks a privilege or debug-mode check, allowing unprivileged AXI reads to retrieve LFSR seed state.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs connected, but the wrapper's internal read path does not demonstrably gate seed outputs on these controls.
evidence.supports_claim: Access-control signals are passed to the wrapper but their application to seed readback is not evidenced in the available source.

evidence.description: rng_top combinatorially assigns seed128 = seed (line 103) and slices it into seed16, seed32, seed64 registers in an always@(*) block without any privilege check. These seed values are also driven to output ports.
evidence.supports_claim: Seed material is held in registers that are unconditionally exposed on output ports.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RNG design routes internal LFSR seed state to module output ports (seed16_o, seed32_o, seed64_o, seed128_o) explicitly labeled for debug use in rng_wrapper. The rng_wrapper AXI read-data multiplexer can return these seed values in response to AXI register reads. While reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i are wired into the wrapper, the available source snippets of the read path (SNIP-0017) show no conditional gate on debug_mode_i or acct_ctrl_i before seed data enters rdata. This constitutes a sensitive-value readback vulnerability: any bus master that can reach the RNG register space can read back the LFSR seed, fully compromising the unpredictability of the random number generator.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access to the RNG peripheral address space can read back the current LFSR seed values for all four entropy sources (16-, 32-, 64-, 128-bit). Knowing the seed and the polynomial (also readable via the same interface) allows complete prediction of all future random numbers produced by the RNG, nullifying any security properties (e.g., nonce generation, key material seeding) that depend on RNG unpredictability.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full rng_wrapper read-mux case statement is not shown in the snippets (only the default case at lines 207-210 is visible). It is possible that earlier case entries gate seed reads on debug_mode_i or acct_ctrl_i, which would mitigate the finding. Additionally, the exact address mapping for seed16_big_o readback is not confirmed in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The RNG wrapper exposes internal seed and debug-mode signals (seed32_big_o, seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed16_big_o) with comments explicitly labeling them as 'signals for debug mode', and the rng_top module exposes seed segment outputs (seed32_o, seed64_o, seed128_o, seed16_o) unconditionally through its port interface. There is no privilege or lock guard preventing readback of the internal LFSR seed state through these debug ports, allowing an unprivileged or debug-mode observer to determine the current RNG seed and predict future random output.

evidence.description: The rng_wrapper declares seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, and seed16_big_o with an explicit comment '// allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode' and '//signals for debug mode'. These signals carry the internal seed state of each LFSR and are wired as readable outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: Internal LFSR seed values are exposed via debug-mode signals with no guarding logic shown in the snippet.

evidence.description: rng_top declares output ports seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o (lines 15-18) that emit the current seed register values for each LFSR unconditionally. These are wired out of the module without any privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: Seed state is unconditionally exported from rng_top, making it readable by any connected observer.

evidence.description: The rng_32 LFSR loads seed_i directly into its entropy32 shift register when load_i is asserted. entropy32_o continuously reflects the shift register state. If the seed can be read back via seed32_o/seed32_big_o, the entire LFSR state is exposed.
evidence.supports_claim: The LFSR state that is readable via debug outputs is identical to the seed after load, enabling full state prediction.

evidence.description: The rng_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals passes reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, suggesting access-control inputs exist. However, there is no evidence in the source snippets that these signals gate the readback of seed debug outputs (seed32_big_o etc.).
evidence.supports_claim: Access control inputs are present at the wrapper boundary but their application to seed readback paths is absent from the provided code evidence.

evidence.description: The rng_top instantiation in rng_wrapper (line 217-219) connects seed32_o to seed32_big_o (per edge:connects:92 and signal:rng_top.seed32_o -> signal:rng_wrapper.seed32_big_o). The reset expression 'rst_n(rst_ni && ~rst_10)' is noteworthy but the critical issue is the unguarded forwarding of seed outputs to debug signals.
evidence.supports_claim: Internal LFSR seed state flows from rng_top seed32_o to rng_wrapper seed32_big_o without any privilege or lock check gating this readback path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RNG subsystem (rng_16/32/64/128 LFSRs) exposes the current seed/state of each LFSR through dedicated output ports (seed16_o, seed32_o, seed64_o, seed128_o) at the rng_top level, which are then connected to 'debug mode' signals (seed16_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed128_big_o) in rng_wrapper. The wrapper comment explicitly labels these as debug-mode readable signals. While the wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i at its ports, no source snippet shows these signals being applied to gate the debug seed readback path. An attacker who can read the debug registers (or who operates in debug mode) can recover the complete LFSR seed state and thereby predict all future random number outputs.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug register interface can read the current seed of each LFSR (16, 32, 64, 128-bit), reconstruct the full internal state, and predict all future outputs of the RNG. This breaks any security protocol relying on the RNG for key generation, nonce generation, or other cryptographic randomness.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full rng_wrapper read-path logic (the always block handling address-based register reads) is not completely shown, so it is uncertain whether reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i are applied to gate reads of the seed debug signals. If they are properly applied, the vulnerability may be mitigated. Additionally, the debug_mode_i signal wiring within rng_wrapper to the seed readback path is not fully visible in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The RNG seed value is exposed as a readable output (seed64_o / seed64_big_o) without privilege or lock enforcement, allowing any bus master to read back the internal LFSR seed and fully compromise the randomness of the RNG subsystem.

evidence.description: rng_top declares seed64_o as an output port alongside debug-oriented per-source seed outputs (seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o) and rand_seg outputs. The comment 'signal for debug mode' indicates these are intended only for debug, yet they are wired out unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: The seed value used to initialize the LFSR is exported as an output from rng_top without any access control applied at this level.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper instantiates rng_top and connects seed64_o to the internal signal seed64_big_o (via edge:connects:91) with no surrounding privilege check or register-lock gate on the readback path. The rdata mux in rng_wrapper's AXI read handler (lines 207-210) contains only a rand_num_valid guard for a default case, with no check of acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i for the seed readback registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The seed readback path from rng_top.seed64_o to rng_wrapper.seed64_big_o is wired directly into the AXI read data path without gating on acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i, meaning any AXI bus master can read the current seed value.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs (lines 19-20) as well as debug_mode_i (line 21), but the seed64_big_o signal (line 49) is declared alongside other seed readback signals with a comment 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode', suggesting access should be conditional on debug_mode_i, yet no such guard is visible in the connected read data path.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control inputs exist in rng_wrapper but are not applied to the seed readback signal seed64_big_o.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates rng_wrapper with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i populated, implying the design intends privilege-gated access to RNG registers. However the instantiation alone does not enforce protection if rng_wrapper's internal logic does not apply these controls to the seed readback path.
evidence.supports_claim: The system-level intent is privilege-gated RNG access, but this intent is not enforced for the seed readback path inside rng_wrapper.

evidence.description: rng_64 loads seed_i directly into entropy64 on load_i assertion (line 35: entropy64 <= seed_i). The entropy64 register is also the LFSR state register, so reading seed64_o reveals the full internal LFSR state, not merely the initial seed.
evidence.supports_claim: Exposure of seed64_o is equivalent to exposing the live LFSR state, allowing an attacker to predict all future random output.

evidence.description: The seed distribution logic in rng_top (lines 101-131) uses a combinational always block that unconditionally assigns seed128 = seed and slices seed into seed16/seed32/seed64 based only on seed_ct. There is no privilege check before these values are computed and forwarded to the sub-RNGs and to the output ports.
evidence.supports_claim: No access control gate is present between the externally supplied seed_i and the seed output ports.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_top module explicitly exposes seed64_o (and seed128_o, seed32_o, seed16_o) as output ports, labeled in comments as 'signal for debug mode'. These ports are connected in rng_wrapper to internal signals (seed64_big_o etc.) and wired into the AXI read-data multiplexer. Although rng_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i, the source snippets show these are not applied to the seed readback path: the seed signals are declared and connected without any conditional gating on privilege level or debug mode. Any AXI bus master with access to the rng_wrapper address range can read back the current LFSR seed, which is identical to the live LFSR state (because seed_i is loaded directly into the shift register). This completely breaks the security of the RNG.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access (e.g., an unprivileged software process if the AXI window is mapped to user space, or any privileged attacker) can read back the full LFSR internal state via the seed readback registers. With the full state of a 64-bit (or 128-bit) LFSR known, all future and—by running the LFSR backward—past random numbers are predictable. This undermines any security property depending on the RNG, including key generation, nonce generation, or randomized countermeasures.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full AXI read-data multiplexer (rdata mux) in rng_wrapper is not completely shown in the snippets; it is possible that some cases in the mux do gate seed readback on acct_ctrl_i or debug_mode_i for specific addresses. The snippets provided show only a default case (lines 207-210) without such gating. The complete address decode table for seed64_big_o is not visible, so a partial mitigation cannot be fully ruled out.

finding.summary: The debug_mode_i signal bypasses the reglk_ctrl_i[7] register-lock protection, allowing full readback of the 128-bit RNG seed (seed128_big_o) and other internal seed/state values when debug mode is active, even if the lock bit is set.

evidence.description: Lines 186-192 show that when debug_mode_i is asserted, all four 32-bit chunks of seed128_big_o are unconditionally returned in rdata, completely bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i[7] lock check. The ternary expression `debug_mode_i ? seed128_big_o[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : seed128_big_o[31:0])` means the lock bit is irrelevant when debug mode is active. The same pattern repeats for seed64, seed32, seed16, and rand_seg outputs (lines 194-202).
evidence.supports_claim: When debug_mode_i is high, the full 128-bit LFSR seed is readable from the register interface regardless of whether reglk_ctrl_i[7] is set, defeating the register-lock protection entirely.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i is declared as an 8-bit input intended to lock register read access (line 19: `input logic [7:0] reglk_ctrl_i`), and debug_mode_i is a separate boolean input (line 21: `input logic debug_mode_i`). There is no hardware interlock preventing debug_mode_i from overriding reglk_ctrl_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The two control inputs exist independently with no subordination relationship enforced in hardware; debug_mode_i can override the lock at any time.

evidence.description: seed128 is the 128-bit seed value loaded into the rng_128 LFSR (line 35: `reg [127:0] seed128`) and is directly exposed as the output seed128_o (line 74: `assign seed128_o = seed128`), which feeds into rng_wrapper.seed128_big_o. This is the actual secret seed material used to initialize the LFSR.
evidence.supports_claim: The seed register holds the secret initial state of the LFSR; readback of this value allows an attacker to reconstruct all future random outputs.

evidence.description: The rng_top instance (line 217) connects seed128_o directly to the wrapper's seed128_big_o signal (via edge:connects:90), making the internal seed continuously readable at the register interface when debug_mode_i is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: The live LFSR seed is wired out to the register readback path with no latching or masking, so asserting debug_mode_i immediately exposes the current seed state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper implements a register-lock mechanism via reglk_ctrl_i[7] to prevent readback of the RNG seed registers. However, the guard logic for seed readback (registers 15-22, lines 186-200 of SNIP-0009) implements a priority ternary where debug_mode_i is checked first, and if true, the seed value is returned unconditionally without evaluating reglk_ctrl_i. This means the lock bit is entirely ineffective when the processor is in debug mode. An attacker or privileged debug session can read the full 128-bit seed, and from there reconstruct all LFSR outputs, breaking the security guarantee of the RNG.
finding.security_impact: Full disclosure of the 128-bit LFSR seed allows complete prediction of all past and future random number outputs. Any cryptographic key material, nonces, or security-sensitive values derived from this RNG are compromised. The register-lock protection mechanism is rendered ineffective by the debug bypass.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets how debug_mode_i is asserted in the system (e.g., whether it requires prior privilege elevation or physical access). If debug_mode_i is only assertable in a trusted debug flow with equivalent privilege to the lock controller, the practical impact is reduced. However, the architectural flaw of debug_mode_i unconditionally overriding the lock is still present in the RTL.

finding.summary: In debug mode, the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i) is bypassed for poly128/poly64/poly32/poly16 register writes in rng_wrapper, AND the seed registers are loaded with fixed constant values rather than random values, exposing the RNG seed and polynomial state to privileged debug access without lock enforcement.

evidence.description: Lines 127-141 show that when debug_mode_i is asserted, the reglk_ctrl_i[5] lock bit is completely bypassed for all poly128, poly64, poly32_big, and poly16_big register writes. The ternary expression 'debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? poly128[3] : wdata[31:0])' means debug mode unconditionally allows writes regardless of the lock register.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i bypasses reglk_ctrl_i lock for RNG polynomial register writes

evidence.description: Lines 106-113 show that when load is asserted and debug_mode_i is NOT set, seed[0..3] are loaded with hardcoded constants (2, 3, 3, 2) rather than any random or external value. This means the RNG seed is predictable and fixed on every load cycle outside debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG seed is set to fixed constants (2,3,3,2) on load, making the seed128_o output predictable

evidence.description: seed128_o is an output port of rng_top that exposes the internal 128-bit seed value. This is the seed target of the chain and is driven directly from seed128 register contents.
evidence.supports_claim: seed128_o exposes the internal RNG seed state externally

evidence.description: Line 74 shows 'assign seed128_o = seed128' - the seed128 register value is directly assigned to the output port with no masking or permission check on the readback path.
evidence.supports_claim: seed128 is unconditionally driven to output port seed128_o without any lock or permission guard

evidence.description: Lines 19-21 show rng_wrapper accepts reglk_ctrl_i (8-bit lock) and debug_mode_i as separate inputs. The comment on line 46 explicitly states 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode', confirming debug mode is intended to bypass normal access controls.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is architecturally designed to override reglk_ctrl_i protections in rng_wrapper

evidence.description: The rng_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals passes debug_mode_i directly from the top-level input without additional gating, meaning any assertion of the system-wide debug_mode_i signal disables RNG register protection.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is propagated without additional privilege gating to rng_wrapper, enabling lock bypass

evidence.description: Lines 217-222 show rng_top is instantiated with seed_i driven from seed_big (assembled from seed[0..3] constants). The seed128_o output is connected back via edge:connects:90 to rng_wrapper.seed128_big_o, making the fixed seed value readable through the peripheral interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Fixed seed constants propagate through rng_top and are exposed on seed128_o output available to the bus

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present: (1) In rng_wrapper's write handler, debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses the reglk_ctrl_i[5] lock bit for all polynomial registers (poly128, poly64, poly32_big, poly16_big), allowing an attacker with debug access to overwrite RNG configuration regardless of lock state. (2) The seed registers are loaded with hardcoded constants (2,3,3,2) on every load event outside debug mode, making the RNG seed deterministic and predictable. The seed128_o signal is then driven unconditionally from these fixed values with no lock/permission guard on the readback path, meaning any reader of seed128_o sees a predictable seed.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can bypass register lock protections to overwrite RNG polynomial feedback taps, controlling the LFSR structure and making outputs predictable. Additionally, the RNG seed is hardcoded to constants (2,3,3,2) on load, breaking entropy: all cryptographic operations dependent on this RNG will use a predictable seed, undermining confidentiality of any key material derived from it.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The rdata readback path for seed128_big_o in rng_wrapper is not fully shown in snippets - it is unclear whether seed128_big_o is gated by debug_mode_i or reglk_ctrl_i on reads. The fixed seed constants may be placeholder/test values rather than intentional design, but the RTL as written has this behavior. The exact privilege level required to assert debug_mode_i is not shown in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The debug_mode_i bypass in rng_wrapper makes the reglk_ctrl_i register-lock protection on seed16 (and other RNG internal seeds) completely ineffective when debug mode is active, allowing sensitive seed material to be read back without any privilege check.

evidence.description: Line 200 shows: rdata = debug_mode_i ? {16'b0, seed16_big_o} : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : {16'b0, seed16_big_o}). When debug_mode_i is asserted, seed16_big_o is returned unconditionally, completely bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i[7] lock bit. The same pattern appears for seed64 (lines 194, 196), seed32 (line 198), and random segment outputs (lines 202, 204, 206).
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses the register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection, exposing internal LFSR seed values including seed16 to any caller who can assert debug_mode_i.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i is an 8-bit register-lock input (line 19) and debug_mode_i is a single-bit input (line 21). The guard logic is structured as a ternary that prioritizes debug_mode_i over reglk_ctrl_i, meaning the lock is only enforced when debug mode is off.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl_i protection mechanism is subordinate to debug_mode_i, so asserting debug mode negates all register-lock protections on sensitive seed readback paths.

evidence.description: seed16 (line 38) and related seeds (seed128, seed64, seed32) are internal LFSR state derived from seed_i. Lines 74-77 assign these internal seed values directly to output ports (seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o) which flow to seed16_big_o in the wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The LFSR seed values are sensitive because they allow reconstruction of the LFSR state and future/past random number outputs.

evidence.description: seed16 is connected from rng_top to RNG16 instance's seed_i (line 188), meaning the seed loaded into the LFSR is directly readable via the debug path.
evidence.supports_claim: The path from seed16 register to entropy source is confirmed, making seed disclosure a complete compromise of the RNG's unpredictability.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper uses a ternary guard pattern 'debug_mode_i ? <sensitive_value> : (reglk_ctrl_i[bit] ? 0 : <sensitive_value>)' for all seed and state readback registers. This means debug_mode_i completely bypasses the register-lock mechanism. If an attacker or privileged debug user can assert debug_mode_i, they can read out all internal LFSR seed values (seed16, seed32, seed64, seed128) regardless of the reglk_ctrl_i lock settings, allowing full reconstruction of RNG internal state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to debug_mode_i can read all internal RNG seed values, completely breaking the unpredictability of random numbers generated by the system. This undermines cryptographic operations, security tokens, or any system component relying on these random numbers for security properties.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is unknown from the provided snippets what controls the assertion of debug_mode_i at the system level (whether it requires a privilege check upstream). If debug_mode_i is properly gated by a secure lifecycle or authentication mechanism externally, the severity would be reduced. However, within the rng_wrapper itself there is no such check visible in the snippets.

finding.summary: Debug mode unconditionally bypasses register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection for RNG polynomial registers, and the seed generation block uses hardcoded constants instead of a random source, undermining the security of the RNG.

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper.sv lines 127-141, writes to poly128, poly64, poly32_big, and poly16_big use the pattern: `debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? poly128[3] : wdata[31:0])`. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the reglk_ctrl_i lock is completely bypassed and the write proceeds unconditionally, allowing any entity asserting debug mode to overwrite RNG polynomial registers regardless of the lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i bypasses reglk_ctrl_i protection on RNG polynomial registers

evidence.description: The intended protection is reglk_ctrl_i[5] gating writes to polynomial registers. However, the guard is entirely skipped when debug_mode_i=1, making the lock ineffective during debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_ctrl_i is the intended write guard but is bypassed by debug_mode_i

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper.sv lines 106-113, the seed generation block assigns hardcoded constants (seed[0]=2, seed[1]=3, seed[2]=3, seed[3]=2) when load is asserted and debug_mode_i is deasserted. This means the RNG seed is not actually random, making the LFSR output predictable from reset.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG seed values are hardcoded constants, not random, making the LFSR output predictable

evidence.description: rng_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and debug_mode_i as inputs. The comment on line 46 states 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode', confirming that debug mode is an intentional but insecure bypass of the lock mechanism.
evidence.supports_claim: Design intentionally allows debug_mode_i to bypass register lock, creating a security hole

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals.sv instantiates rng_wrapper and passes debug_mode_i directly from the top-level input port without any additional filtering or privilege check before it reaches the RNG wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is propagated from the processor debug interface directly to rng_wrapper without additional access control

evidence.description: seed16_o is continuously assigned from the seed16 register which is derived from seed_i and output directly. This seed value flows to rng_16 as seed_i and is also exported as an observable output, making the internal seed state readable.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG seed state (seed16_o) is exposed as an output signal

evidence.description: rng_top is instantiated in rng_wrapper with seed_big derived from the hardcoded seed array, and seed16_big_o is connected back from rng_top.seed16_o, making the predictable seed observable at the wrapper boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: Predictable seed value is observable at module boundary via seed16_big_o output

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present. First, in rng_wrapper.sv, the write-side register map uses `debug_mode_i ? wdata : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? retain : wdata)`, meaning asserting debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses the reglk_ctrl lock for all polynomial register writes. An attacker or privileged debug agent can overwrite the LFSR polynomials even when the lock bits are set, fully controlling the RNG output. Second, the seed initialization block hardcodes seed[0..3] to constants {2,3,3,2} rather than using any entropy source, making the LFSR output deterministically predictable from power-on. The combination means an attacker who knows debug mode is active can both predict and manipulate the RNG output.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can bypass register locks to overwrite RNG polynomial registers, enabling full control over RNG output. Additionally, the hardcoded seed values make the RNG output predictable without any special access, undermining any cryptographic use of the random numbers (e.g., key generation, nonce production). The seed is also exposed as an output signal (seed16_o/seed16_big_o), enabling seed readback.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to assert debug_mode_i is not shown in the provided snippets; if debug_mode_i is only assertable by M-mode or a trusted debug controller, the impact may be partially mitigated for external attackers, but the lock bypass remains a design flaw. The read-side access control for seed registers under reglk_ctrl is not shown in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The seed32 readback path in rng_wrapper exposes the RNG seed value identically in both debug_mode and non-debug_mode (when reglk_ctrl_i[7] is 0), meaning the lock bit provides no additional protection compared to debug mode, and the debug_mode bypass is redundant/ineffective as a security boundary.

evidence.description: At line 198, the rdata assignment for address 21 (seed32_big_o) is: `rdata = debug_mode_i ? seed32_big_o : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : seed32_big_o)`. When debug_mode_i is 0 and reglk_ctrl_i[7] is 0, seed32_big_o is returned in plaintext. The debug_mode path returns seed32_big_o unconditionally, and the non-debug path also returns seed32_big_o when the register lock bit is not set. This means the debug_mode check provides no meaningful access restriction—both paths return the same sensitive seed value when the lock is open. The same pattern appears at lines 192, 194, 196, 200, 202, 204, 206 for seed128, seed64, seed16, rand_seg, and cs_state outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i bypass is supposed to grant privileged access to seed and state values, but the non-debug path returns identical data when reglk_ctrl_i[7]=0, making debug_mode a non-protection. Additionally, the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i[7]) is the only barrier for normal operation, and when unlocked, sensitive RNG seed values including seed32 are fully readable by any bus master.

evidence.description: signal:rng_wrapper.reglk_ctrl_i is declared as an 8-bit register lock input, and signal:rng_wrapper.debug_mode_i is a single-bit debug mode flag. Both are inputs to the wrapper, used to gate readback of sensitive seed and entropy signals.
evidence.supports_claim: The two protection signals exist but their combined logic in the read mux is flawed: debug_mode grants unconditional seed readback, but normal mode with unlocked registers also grants full seed readback, making the debug_mode distinction meaningless from a security perspective.

evidence.description: seed32 is a 32-bit register in rng_top (line 37) holding a segment of the RNG seed. It is assigned from the external seed_i input via a counter-based rotation and fed directly to the rng_32 LFSR instance. It is exported as seed32_o (line 76) and connected to rng_wrapper.seed32_big_o.
evidence.supports_claim: seed32 is a sensitive internal seed value that is directly exposed as a readable output, and the wrapper's access control for this value is ineffective when register lock bit 7 is clear.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper read mux at line 198 (and surrounding lines) uses a ternary pattern: `debug_mode_i ? <secret> : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : <secret>)`. This means debug mode and unlocked normal mode both return the same sensitive seed/state data. The debug_mode_i signal provides no incremental protection over the register lock alone. When reglk_ctrl_i[7]=0 (registers unlocked), seed32_big_o (and other seed/state values) are fully readable by any AXI bus master without requiring debug mode. The design intent appears to be that debug mode should be the only way to access these sensitive internal values, but the implementation makes them equally accessible in normal unlocked mode.
finding.security_impact: Any software running with AXI bus access can read back the internal RNG seed values (seed32, seed16, seed64, seed128, rand_seg, cs_state) when the register lock bit is not set. Knowledge of the seed allows an attacker to predict all future outputs of the LFSR-based RNG, breaking any security that relies on RNG unpredictability (e.g., ASLR, key generation, nonce generation).
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown from the snippets what controls reglk_ctrl_i[7] or whether it is set by default after reset. If reglk_ctrl_i[7] is always set to 1 in the system, the impact is reduced. However, the structural vulnerability in the debug_mode vs. normal-mode logic equivalence is clear from the source.

finding.summary: The RNG wrapper's seed register write path and polynomial register write path grant debug mode unconditional write access, bypassing the register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection. Additionally, the seed initialization logic uses hardcoded constant values, making the RNG seed predictable and non-random.

evidence.description: Lines 127-141 show that when debug_mode_i is asserted, writes to poly128, poly64, poly32_big, and poly16_big bypass the reglk_ctrl_i[5] lock check entirely. The ternary expression 'debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? poly128[3] : wdata[31:0])' means that in debug mode, the lock bit is ignored and the register is always overwritten with attacker-controlled wdata.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode unconditionally bypasses the register-lock protection on polynomial registers used to configure the LFSR/RNG, allowing an attacker in debug mode to set arbitrary polynomials and control RNG output.

evidence.description: Lines 106-113 show the seed initialization block: when load & ~debug_mode_i, the seeds are hardcoded constants (seed[0]=2, seed[1]=3, seed[2]=3, seed[3]=2). These are fixed, predictable values rather than any entropy source, making the RNG seed entirely predictable when loaded outside debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: The RNG seed is hardcoded to constant values (2, 3, 3, 2), meaning the initial internal state of the RNG is fully predictable, undermining the security of any cryptographic or random-number-dependent operation.

evidence.description: The rng_wrapper module declares reglk_ctrl_i as an 8-bit register lock input and debug_mode_i as a debug mode flag. The seed and polynomial registers are intended to be protected by these controls.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals connects reglk_ctrl[10*8+7:10*8] and debug_mode_i to the rng_wrapper instance, establishing the intended permission controls for the RNG peripheral.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities exist in rng_wrapper.sv. First, the write guard for polynomial registers uses 'debug_mode_i ? wdata : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? current_val : wdata)', which means debug mode completely bypasses the register lock, allowing unconstrained writes to LFSR polynomial configuration registers. Second, when the load signal is asserted outside debug mode, the seed registers are set to hardcoded constants {2,3,3,2} rather than any entropy source, making the RNG initial state fully predictable. Together, these issues mean the RNG is cryptographically weak: its seed is predictable and its polynomial can be overridden in debug mode.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can override the LFSR polynomial registers to any value, effectively controlling the structure of the RNG output. Even without debug mode, the RNG seed is hardcoded, making all generated random numbers predictable from reset. Any cryptographic operation (e.g., key generation, nonce generation) relying on this RNG is compromised, with outputs that are either constant/predictable or attacker-controlled.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-path guard logic for seed readback registers (seed32_big_o etc.) is not shown in the snippets, so it is unclear whether debug mode also bypasses read locks. The acct_ctrl_i signal's role in gating access is referenced but its effect on the write path is not visible in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The RNG seed value (seed64) is readable via rdata when debug_mode_i is asserted, bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i register lock. Additionally, even when not in debug mode, the lock check uses only reglk_ctrl_i[7] for all seed readback registers (19-24), meaning a single bit controls all seed segment readback. evidence.description: Lines 194 and 196 show that when debug_mode_i is asserted, seed64_big_o is returned directly in rdata without any register-lock check. The ternary expression 'debug_mode_i ? seed64_big_o[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : seed64_big_o[31:0])' completely bypasses reglk_ctrl_i when debug_mode_i=1.

evidence.description: rng_top exposes seed64_o as an output specifically labeled as a 'signal for debug mode / seed segment for each entropy source', confirming this is sensitive key material intended to be protected.

evidence.description: The guard logic pattern 'debug_mode_i ? <sensitive_data> : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : <sensitive_data>)' means reglk_ctrl_i[7]=1 only blocks readback in non-debug mode. When debug_mode_i=1, the lock is entirely ignored and sensitive data flows to rdata unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock guard is defeated by debug_mode_i, confirming the bypass vulnerability.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper module implements a pattern where debug_mode_i takes priority over the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i) for seed readback. When debug_mode_i=1, raw seed values (seed64, seed128, seed32, seed16, and LFSR segment outputs) are returned directly in rdata without checking reglk_ctrl_i. This means any software or hardware entity that can assert the debug_mode_i signal can read back the internal RNG seed values regardless of register lock configuration. The seed values themselves are the LFSR initial states, knowledge of which allows predicting all future RNG outputs.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious software that can assert debug_mode_i can read back the full RNG seed (all segments: 128-bit, 64-bit, 32-bit, 16-bit) through the AXI register interface, completely defeating the confidentiality of the LFSR state. This allows full prediction of all future random numbers generated by the RNG, undermining any security that depends on random number unpredictability (e.g., ASLR, nonces, cryptographic keys derived from RNG output).
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source does not show how debug_mode_i is driven or what privilege level is required to assert it. If debug_mode_i is hardware-controlled and only asserted in a genuine trusted debug context (e.g., JTAG with proper authentication), the risk may be acceptable by design. However, the bypass is unconditional in RTL with no additional guard shown.

finding.summary: In rng_wrapper, debug_mode_i bypasses the reglk_ctrl register-lock protection for polynomial writes, and the seed register is initialized to constant/hardcoded values rather than truly random values when a load signal arrives outside debug mode, exposing sensitive RNG state.

evidence.description: Lines 127-141 show that when debug_mode_i is asserted, writes to poly128[], poly64[], poly32_big, and poly16_big bypass the reglk_ctrl_i register-lock check entirely: 'poly128[3] <= debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? poly128[3] : wdata[31:0])'. This means any entity that can assert debug_mode_i can freely overwrite all polynomial (LFSR feedback) values regardless of the lock register state.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i unconditionally bypasses register-lock protection for all polynomial registers in the RNG

evidence.description: Lines 106-113 show the seed initialization block: 'if(load & ~debug_mode_i) begin seed[0] <= 2; seed[1] <= 3; seed[2] <= 3; seed[3] <= 2; end'. The seeds are hardcoded constants (2, 3, 3, 2), not random values. The comment says 'assume the seed generation is random' but it is not. Also note this only fires when NOT in debug mode, meaning in debug mode no seed update occurs at all from this block.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG seed is initialized to fixed/constant values, not random values, making the LFSR output predictable

evidence.description: rng_top exposes seed64_o, seed128_o, seed32_o, seed16_o as outputs labeled 'signal for debug mode / seed segment for each entropy source', which flow back through rng_wrapper as seed64_big_o etc. These outputs expose the internal LFSR seed state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper module contains two concrete security issues evidenced in the source code. First, debug_mode_i unconditionally overrides the reglk_ctrl_i register-lock for all polynomial register writes (lines 127-141 of rng_wrapper.sv), meaning any attacker or firmware that can assert debug_mode_i can freely reconfigure the LFSR polynomials and thus predict or control RNG output regardless of the lock register. Second, when a load pulse is received (and not in debug mode), the seed registers are set to fixed constants {2, 3, 3, 2} rather than actual random values (lines 106-113), directly contradicting the comment that says 'assume the seed generation is random'. The seed values are also exported as observable outputs (seed64_o, seed128_o, etc.) through rng_top back to rng_wrapper without a read-lock guard, enabling full seed state readback in debug mode.
finding.security_impact: An attacker able to assert debug_mode_i (e.g., via a privileged debug interface) can: (1) overwrite all LFSR polynomials to control RNG output, (2) read back the full LFSR seed state via seed64_big_o/seed128_big_o outputs, and (3) since the initial seed is hardcoded to constants, the RNG output is deterministically predictable from reset until a proper external seed is provided, undermining all cryptographic operations (key generation, nonces, etc.) that depend on this RNG.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The read-side lock logic for seed readback registers is not fully shown in the snippets; it is possible that seed64_big_o reads are gated elsewhere in the rdata multiplexer not shown. However, the polynomial write bypass via debug_mode_i and the hardcoded seed initialization are directly evidenced in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: RNG seed values (seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o) and internal LFSR state are exposed as debug outputs without any privilege or lock guard in rng_top, and the rng_wrapper exposes these signals labeled 'for debug mode' with no observed access control gating on the readback path.

evidence.description: rng_top exposes seed128_o, seed64_o, seed32_o, seed16_o as output ports, explicitly commented as 'signal for debug mode / seed segment for each entropy source'. These carry the exact seed values loaded into each LFSR.
evidence.supports_claim: The internal seed state of all four LFSRs is directly output on named debug ports with no masking.

evidence.description: Lines 74-77 unconditionally assign the internal seed registers to the debug output ports: 'assign seed128_o = seed128; assign seed64_o = seed64; assign seed32_o = seed32; assign seed16_o = seed16;' There is no conditional guard (e.g., debug_mode_i, reglk_ctrl_i) protecting these assignments.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper declares inputs reglk_ctrl_i (register lock), acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i (line 19-21), and the seed output signals seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed16_big_o are declared internally (lines 48-51) with the comment 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode'. finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_top module unconditionally exports the current seed values of all four LFSR instances (128-bit, 64-bit, 32-bit, 16-bit) via output ports labeled as debug signals (seed128_o etc.). The assignments at lines 74-77 of rng_top.v have no conditional logic gating them on debug_mode_i, reglk_ctrl_i, or any privilege signal. The rng_wrapper does declare debug_mode_i and reglk_ctrl_i inputs, but the provided source snippets show no evidence that these are applied to gate the seed output readback path before it reaches the AXI rdata bus. This means any software with AXI access to the RNG peripheral can read back the exact seed loaded into each LFSR, defeating the unpredictability of the random number generator.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the AXI bus interface can read back the current LFSR seed values for all four entropy sources. Since LFSR output is fully deterministic from its seed and polynomial, knowing the seed allows complete prediction of all future random numbers generated, breaking the security properties of any cryptographic or security-sensitive use of this RNG.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The AXI read-path code in rng_wrapper that actually drives rdata from seed128_big_o etc. is not fully shown in the snippets (only a partial default case is visible). It is possible that debug_mode_i or reglk_ctrl_i gates the rdata assignment in a part of the file not included. However, the unconditional seed output assignments in rng_top (lines 74-77) are confirmed, and the wrapper comment explicitly says these signals are readable in debug mode without showing any non-debug suppression.

finding.summary: The RNG seed counter (seed_ct) and the LFSR seed registers in rng_top reset synchronously only on rst_n (active-low), but the RNG is instantiated with a combined reset expression `rst_ni && ~rst_10`. This means that asserting rst_10 (a per-peripheral software-triggered reset) drives rst_n low, clearing entropy state and reseeding the LFSRs with zero or a software-supplied seed_i with no privilege check at the wrapper level. Additionally, the RNG wrapper exposes internal seed values (seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, etc.) as outputs in debug mode without a concrete guard shown in the snippets — the comment says 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode', suggesting the debug_mode_i flag enables sensitive seed readback, but no explicit conditional blocking of the readback path is demonstrated in the available code.

evidence.description: rng_top is instantiated with `.rst_n(rst_ni && ~rst_10)`, meaning the per-peripheral software reset signal rst_10 can independently force rst_n low, resetting all LFSR state (entropy128, entropy64, entropy32, entropy16) to zero. This allows any entity that can write rst_10 to destroy RNG state and force re-seeding.
evidence.supports_claim: Unsafe reset behavior: a software-accessible peripheral reset (rst_10) can clear the cryptographic RNG seed state without additional privilege checks.

evidence.description: The rng_wrapper declares seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed16_big_o as internal signals and a comment at line 46 states 'allow user to modify poly and read signals below on debug mode', with debug_mode_i as an input. This indicates that in debug mode, internal seed values of the RNG are readable, constituting a sensitive readback path.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i enables readback of internal RNG seed values, which are security-sensitive for a cryptographic entropy source.

evidence.description: seed_ct is a 2-bit counter in rng_top that tracks the seeding state of the RNG. It resets to 0 on rst_n deassertion (synchronous on posedge clk with if(!rst_n)). Since rst_n can be cleared by rst_10, seed_ct is vulnerable to forced reinitialization.
evidence.supports_claim: The seed counter controlling RNG seeding lifecycle is cleared whenever rst_10 is asserted, undermining the integrity of the seeding process.

evidence.description: rng_128 resets entropy128 to 128'b0 on negedge rst_n. Since rst_n is driven by rst_ni && ~rst_10, asserting rst_10 resets the LFSR state to the all-zeros weak state, which is a degenerate state for an LFSR.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing rst_n low via rst_10 resets all LFSR entropy to zero, a pathologically weak seed state for all RNG sub-modules.

evidence.description: The rng_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs, indicating register-lock and account-control mechanisms exist. However, no snippet shows these controls blocking the rst_10-triggered reset or the debug-mode seed readback path.
evidence.supports_claim: The existing permission controls (reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i) are not shown to protect the reset or debug readback paths for the RNG seed state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_top module is instantiated in rng_wrapper with its reset driven by `rst_ni && ~rst_10`. The software-accessible rst_10 signal can independently assert reset, clearing all LFSR seed state to zero (a degenerate LFSR state) and resetting the seed_ct counter. This constitutes an unsafe reset vulnerability since an unprivileged or malicious actor with register write access to the peripheral reset register can destroy RNG entropy state. Furthermore, the wrapper exposes internal seed outputs under debug_mode_i with no observable guard in the snippets beyond a comment, constituting a potential sensitive readback vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the peripheral reset register can force the RNG into a fully known (all-zero) LFSR state, making generated 'random' numbers predictable until fresh entropy is loaded. Debug mode enables readback of the actual seed values used by the RNG, directly compromising forward secrecy of any cryptographic operations depending on this entropy source.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of the debug-mode seed readback gating logic is not shown in the available snippets — it is possible that debug_mode_i properly gates the seed output signals elsewhere in rng_wrapper. The privilege level required to assert rst_10 is also not fully visible from the provided snippets. The acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i protection scope may or may not cover the reset path.

finding.summary: The RNG seed registers are hardcoded to constant values (2, 3, 3, 2) during the seed-load operation, and debug_mode_i bypasses register-lock protection to expose seed values via readback, while the seed generation itself produces a predictable, non-random seed.

evidence.description: When the load signal is asserted and debug_mode_i is deasserted, the seed registers are unconditionally set to hardcoded constant values: seed[0]=2, seed[1]=3, seed[2]=3, seed[3]=2. The comment claims 'the seed generation is random' but no actual random source is used. This produces a fully predictable, constant seed (0x00000002_00000003_00000003_00000002) for the LFSR-based RNG, making all generated random numbers deterministic and predictable.
evidence.supports_claim: The seed loaded into the RNG is a hardcoded constant rather than a random value, making the entire RNG output deterministic and predictable by any observer.

evidence.description: When debug_mode_i is asserted, the readback path unconditionally returns the raw seed register values (seed[0] through seed[3]) regardless of the reglk_ctrl_i register-lock bits. The guard 'reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? 0 : seed[x]' is entirely bypassed by debug_mode_i. An attacker or privileged debug user can read back the seed material even when register lock is engaged.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i acts as a complete bypass of the register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection for seed readback, allowing sensitive seed values to be read when register lock should prevent it.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i is declared as the register-lock control input (8 bits) and debug_mode_i is a separate input. The seed_big signal is formed by concatenating all four seed registers and passed directly to rng_top. The register-lock is meant to protect seed readback but is bypassed in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock mechanism exists but is ineffective when debug_mode_i is active, and the seed values themselves are constants regardless.

evidence.description: seed_big (concatenation of the four constant seed registers) is passed directly to rng_top as seed_i with no additional privilege check or randomness injection at the instantiation boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: The constant seed propagates unchecked into the RNG core, confirming the predictable seed reaches the LFSR entropy sources.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two distinct vulnerabilities are present. First, the seed 'generation' block (SNIP-0009) assigns hardcoded constants (2, 3, 3, 2) to the seed registers rather than any actual entropy source, making the entire LFSR-based RNG fully predictable regardless of inputs. Second, the readback logic (SNIP-0010) uses debug_mode_i as a complete override of the reglk_ctrl_i register-lock for seed register readback, meaning an attacker with debug access can always read the seed values even when the register lock should prevent disclosure. The combination means the seed is both predictable and readable.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i can bypass register-lock protection and read back the RNG seed. Additionally, because the seed is a hardcoded constant, any party with knowledge of the RTL can predict all random number outputs without needing debug access. This completely undermines the security properties of the RNG for any cryptographic or security-sensitive use (e.g., key generation, nonce generation).
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that the seed registers are also written via the AXI write path (not fully shown in snippets), which could override the hardcoded values. However, the shown seed-load path explicitly uses only the hardcoded constants, and no entropy source is visible in the provided snippets. The debug_mode_i bypass of reglk_ctrl_i is unambiguous in SNIP-0010.

finding.summary: In rng_wrapper, the access-control gating for the AXI enable signal is missing: en_acct (raw AXI enable) is passed directly to the AXI lite interface instead of the gated en signal, allowing RNG register reads and writes to proceed regardless of acct_ctrl_i.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper declares both en (gated) and en_acct (raw from AXI interface). The AXI lite interface outputs its enable to en_acct on line 57, while en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i is the properly gated version.

evidence.description: Line 57 shows en and en_acct declared together, and the graph edge edge:connects:76 shows en_acct (not en) is wired to axi_lite_interface.en_o. The gated signal en is defined as en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, but the AXI interface receives en_acct back, not en.

evidence.description: In pkt_wrapper line 68, assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i correctly gates the AXI enable with the access-control input before using en in register read/write logic. This pattern is correct.
evidence.supports_claim: pkt_wrapper correctly gates en with acct_ctrl_i before using it, confirming the intended design pattern and highlighting that rng_wrapper wires en_acct (ungated) to its AXI lite interface instead.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper exposes RNG seed values, polynomial tap values, random number outputs, and RNG state (cs_state_o) through its register interface. These are security-sensitive cryptographic values.
evidence.supports_claim: The register space protected (or meant to be protected) by acct_ctrl_i includes RNG seed readback and configuration, which are sensitive.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated with acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][10], meaning access should be restricted based on privilege level. However, because en_acct bypasses acct_ctrl_i inside rng_wrapper, this privilege check is ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege-level-based access control fed into acct_ctrl_i has no effect on actual RNG register access because the wrapper uses the ungated en_acct signal.

evidence.description: Edge edge:connects:76 confirms rng_wrapper.en_acct connects to axi_lite_interface.en_o. The rng_wrapper internal register logic should use en (gated by acct_ctrl_i) but the AXI lite output is en_acct. Without seeing the full register read/write always block it is likely that en_acct (not en) gates register access, or en is computed but never used to gate the AXI transaction enable fed back into the interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The ungated en_acct signal is the one connected to the AXI interface enable output, meaning all AXI transactions to the RNG are enabled regardless of acct_ctrl_i.

finding.reasoning_summary: In pkt_wrapper the pattern is: AXI interface outputs en_acct, then assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i gates it, and register logic uses en. In rng_wrapper, the same two signals (en, en_acct) exist and acct_ctrl_i is present, but the graph edge (edge:connects:76) shows rng_wrapper.en_acct connected to axi_lite_interface.en_o — meaning the AXI interface enable output goes to en_acct. The internal register always-blocks then need to use en (gated) not en_acct. However, SNIP-0014 does not show the full always block body for rng_wrapper registers, so we cannot confirm exactly what gates the register writes. What is confirmed is that the AXI lite interface enable output (en_o) is mapped to en_acct, not en, which is the ungated path. finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper implements a register-lock scheme via reglk_ctrl_i and a debug bypass via debug_mode_i. The source code (SNIP-0010) shows that for seed and polynomial register reads, debug_mode_i completely overrides the reglk_ctrl_i protection, allowing full readback of seed and polynomial values. For writes to polynomial registers, the same bypass applies. Additionally, the seed write logic (lines 104-113) sets seeds to fixed predictable constants (2,3,3,2) whenever load fires and debug_mode_i is low, without consulting reglk_ctrl_i at all. These two issues together mean: (1) an attacker with debug mode access can exfiltrate seed and polynomial values, and (2) the seed registers can be deterministically overwritten with known-constant values, making the RNG output predictable. The inconsistent application of debug_mode_i across different register addresses further indicates a poorly reasoned protection model.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode privileges can read all RNG seed and polynomial register values, recovering the full LFSR internal state and making all future random number outputs predictable. The fixed-constant seed initialization means even without debug access, the RNG starts with a known state each load cycle, producing predictable output until sufficient entropy mixing occurs. This undermines any cryptographic or security use of the RNG.

The actual entropy sources feeding the LFSR are not shown, so the severity of the fixed-seed issue depends on whether mixing with external entropy occurs. The acct_ctrl_i access control signal behavior in the broader system is not fully visible.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper module receives acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i to enforce privilege-based and lock-based access control on its registers. However, the axi_lite_interface submodule, which sits between the AXI bus and the register file, drives we_o (write enable) and data_o (write data) directly from AXI request signals without any conditioning on these permission inputs. The en_acct signal shows an intent to differentiate gated from ungated enable, but the write path (wdata and we) is not gated. This means any AXI master can write to the RNG seed and polynomial registers regardless of privilege level or lock state, allowing injection of a known seed and destroying RNG unpredictability.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI master can write attacker-controlled values to the RNG seed and polynomial registers, forcing the LFSR-based RNG to produce predictable output. This undermines any cryptographic protocol or key generation that relies on this RNG for entropy.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of rng_wrapper's register-write logic (the always_comb block that actually writes seed registers from wdata conditioned on we/en_acct) is not fully shown in the snippets. It is possible that en_acct (rather than raw en) gates the actual register update inside rng_wrapper's combinational logic, which would mitigate the finding. However, based on the available snippets, the axi_lite_interface drives we_o and data_o unconditionally, and the distinction between en and en_acct in SNIP-0018 strongly suggests the write-enable path is not properly guarded.

finding.summary: mod_exp_reset is hardwired to 1'b0, permanently disabling the reset capability of the modular exponentiation unit in the RSA core.

evidence.description: Line 18 declares `wire mod_exp_reset = 1'b0;`, hardcoding the reset signal to logic zero. This means the modular exponentiation submodule can never be reset through this signal, regardless of system state or security requirements.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset control for the RSA modular exponentiation unit is permanently disabled, removing the ability to clear sensitive intermediate cryptographic state (exponent, modulo, message registers) by asserting this reset.

evidence.description: Lines 23-28 show registers exp_reg, msg_reg, and mod_reg that hold sensitive cryptographic material (exponent, modulus, message). These registers are clocked but can never be cleared via mod_exp_reset since it is always 0.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive RSA key material and plaintext held in exp_reg, msg_reg, mod_reg cannot be securely wiped via the reset path, leaving residual secret data accessible after an operation completes.

finding.reasoning_summary: The signal `mod_exp_reset` is intended to reset the modular exponentiation unit within the RSA core. However, it is unconditionally tied to 1'b0 via a constant wire assignment, meaning no reset can ever be triggered. This is a security-relevant issue because: (1) the RSA core processes highly sensitive key material (private exponent d, prime factors p and q, modulus), (2) registers exp_reg, mod_reg, and msg_reg retain this sensitive data across operations, and (3) without the ability to assert a reset, there is no mechanism to clear this data on demand, for instance after a security lifecycle event or between tenants.
finding.security_impact: Sensitive RSA private key material (exponent, modulus, message) held in pipeline registers cannot be cleared via the reset mechanism. This leaves residual cryptographic secrets in hardware registers, potentially exposable through side-channels, fault attacks, or if the hardware is accessed by another party after an RSA operation completes. It also defeats any software-directed secure key erasure policy.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full module instantiation and any higher-level reset logic are not provided, so it is theoretically possible that mod_exp_reset is unused and reset is handled elsewhere. However, the comment on lines 21-22 explicitly discusses the purpose of this signal for resetting the module, and its constant-zero assignment is clearly a bug or design flaw rather than intentional dead code.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The source snippet shows a straightforward pipeline register assignment: msg_reg <= msg_in at every positive clock edge. There is no missing guard, bypassed lock, missing authentication/privilege check, unsafe reset behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update visible in this chain. The assignment is an unconditional clocked register capture of the input message, which is normal RTL behavior for a one-cycle delay pipeline stage feeding the modular exponentiation module. No permission-related vulnerability is evidenced by the available source code.

finding.summary: The debug_mode_i signal in rng_wrapper bypasses the register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection for sensitive RNG seed and polynomial registers, allowing unrestricted read and write access when debug mode is active.

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper, write operations to polynomial registers (poly128, poly64, poly32_big, poly16_big) use the pattern: 'debug_mode_i ? wdata : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? retain_value : wdata)'. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the reglk_ctrl_i lock is entirely bypassed and the write proceeds unconditionally. Similarly, read operations for seed and polynomial registers use: 'debug_mode_i ? sensitive_value : (reglk_ctrl_i[N] ? 0 : sensitive_value)'. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the lock bit is ignored and the sensitive data is returned in plaintext.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i unconditionally overrides the reglk_ctrl_i register-lock protection, enabling both reads of sensitive seed/polynomial material and writes to locked registers without privilege or lock enforcement.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with debug_mode_i passed directly as an input, and acct_ctrl_i is derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][10]. The debug_mode_i input is a separate, unguarded bypass path into the module.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is an external input to rng_wrapper that, when asserted, bypasses all register-lock protections enforced by reglk_ctrl_i.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]), forming the access control values used for acct_ctrl_i. These are the gating values that control peripheral access via the en signal, but they do not affect the debug_mode_i bypass path.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control system (acc_ctrl_c) does not constrain the debug_mode_i bypass, confirming the bypass is independent of normal privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: In rng_wrapper, en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i controls whether any register operation proceeds. However, once en is asserted, the debug_mode_i signal inside the write and read logic independently bypasses the reglk_ctrl_i checks for individual registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The en-gate enforces access control at the peripheral level, but within an authorized session the debug_mode_i signal removes the per-register lock protection.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper similarly receives acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i, and contains RSA prime and message registers. The parallel structure to rng_wrapper suggests the same debug bypass pattern likely applies to RSA cryptographic material.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive cryptographic registers (RSA primes, RNG seeds, polynomial values) are the protected resources that the debug_mode_i bypass exposes.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_wrapper module uses debug_mode_i as a ternary select that, when true, directly returns or writes sensitive register values (seeds, polynomials) without checking the corresponding reglk_ctrl_i lock bits. This pattern appears throughout both the write side (lines 127-141) and read side (lines 156-194) of the register map. The normal protection path requires reglk_ctrl_i to be clear for access, but the debug override skips this check entirely. Any software or hardware path that can assert debug_mode_i gains unrestricted access to all locked RNG registers regardless of the lock state. finding.reasoning_summary: The RSA wrapper receives a dedicated rst_13 signal sourced from a software-accessible rst_wrapper peripheral. This signal can reset the RSA core's internal state including prime keys and message buffers. Neither the instantiation in riscv_peripherals nor the rsa_wrapper module itself applies reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i checks before allowing rst_13 to affect internal state. The same unguarded reset pattern is visible in pkt_wrapper (rst_5) and rng_wrapper (rst_10). An attacker with access to the rst_wrapper register interface can assert rst_13 to clear RSA in-progress computations or sensitive key material regardless of privilege level, bypassing the access-control and register-lock mechanisms that protect normal register accesses.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious software actor that can write to the rst_wrapper peripheral's register interface can assert rst_13 to reset the RSA peripheral at will. This can: (1) abort in-progress RSA operations to cause denial of service, (2) clear sensitive prime key material loaded into the RSA engine before it is consumed, (3) force the RSA engine back to a known initial state enabling fault-injection style attacks on cryptographic operations. The reset bypass is not gated by any of the register-lock (reglk_ctrl) or access-control (acct_ctrl) signals that otherwise protect RSA register accesses.

It is possible that rst_13 is combined with rst_ni internally (e.g., inter_rst_ni = rst_ni && ~rst_13) and that combination is subject to further checks not visible here. Additionally, the rst_wrapper peripheral's own access controls are not shown, so it is unclear whether rst_13 assertion is itself privilege-gated at that level. Confidence is medium rather than high due to these gaps.
