finding.summary: The rng_top module reset is computed as (rst_ni && ~rst_10), meaning an external rst_10 signal can independently reset the RNG entropy state without going through the normal system reset path, potentially allowing an attacker with access to rst_10 to force the RNG into a known/predictable state.

evidence.description: rng_top is instantiated with .rst_n(rst_ni && ~rst_10), where rst_10 is a separate input that can assert reset independently of the system rst_ni. This allows the RNG LFSR state to be reset to its seed values at any time by asserting rst_10, bypassing the normal lifecycle reset control.
evidence.supports_claim: An external signal (rst_10) can force the RNG into a known reset state independently of the system reset, enabling entropy state manipulation.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper holds internal seed and polynomial state (seed[], poly128[], poly64[], poly32_big, poly16_big) that feeds the RNG. Resetting rng_top via rst_10 reloads these values, making the RNG output predictable.
evidence.supports_claim: The RNG entropy state is a security-sensitive resource that should only be reset under controlled conditions.

evidence.description: rng_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with rst_ni and reglk_ctrl_i, but rst_10 is also passed as a separate input. The presence of rst_10 as an independent reset path is not gated by any privilege or lock check visible in the snippets.
evidence.supports_claim: The secondary reset path (rst_10) is not subject to the same access control as the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i).

evidence.description: rst_13 is declared as a separate logic signal alongside other numbered reset signals (rst_6 through rst_13), suggesting a pattern of per-peripheral secondary resets that may be externally controllable.
evidence.supports_claim: The numbered reset signals (rst_10, rst_13, etc.) appear to be a design pattern of secondary resets not tied to the main permission/lock infrastructure.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rng_top instantiation in rng_wrapper uses a compound reset expression rst_ni && ~rst_10. The rst_10 input is a separate signal passed into rng_wrapper from riscv_peripherals. Asserting rst_10 forces rng_top (and all its sub-RNGs: rng_128, rng_64, rng_32, rng_16) into reset, clearing their LFSR state back to the loaded seed. This is not gated by reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i. An entity that can control rst_10 can repeatedly reset the RNG to a known state, making its output predictable and breaking entropy guarantees.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert rst_10 can force the RNG entropy source into a known state at will, making all subsequent random numbers predictable. This undermines any cryptographic use of the RNG output (e.g., key generation, nonce generation).
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of rst_10 (what drives it in riscv_peripherals) is not fully shown in the snippets—SNIP-0003 shows rst_13 declared but the driver of rst_10 is not visible. If rst_10 is only driven by a trusted hardware reset controller with equivalent privilege requirements, the severity would be reduced. The rng_wrapper body showing how rst_10 is used in register logic is also not fully shown beyond the rng_top instantiation.

finding.summary: The rsa_wrapper routes AXI write data (wdata) directly to the axi_lite_interface without any reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl gating on the write-data path itself. Although reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i signals are present as inputs, the axi_lite_interface that processes write transactions does not receive or check these permission signals; it unconditionally forwards write data to the peripheral registers whenever AXI write handshaking completes.

evidence.description: rsa_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i as inputs but the axi_lite_interface instantiation at line 46-51 does not have these permission signals connected to it. The wdata signal (line 39) flows directly from the AXI write channel to the axi_lite_interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Permission signals reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i are not passed into or checked by the axi_lite_interface that handles actual write transactions.

evidence.description: The axi_lite_interface module ports (lines 16-32) include no permission/lock/access-control inputs. It only receives axi_req_i, clk, rst, and produces address_o, en_o, we_o, data_o. There is no mechanism to suppress writes based on reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl.
evidence.supports_claim: axi_lite_interface has no guard logic for register lock or access control; any AXI write request that passes AXI handshaking results in we_o=1 and data_o carrying untransformed wdata.

evidence.description: In the WRITE state (lines 114-123), when axi_req_i.w_valid is asserted, en_o and we_o are unconditionally set to 1 and data_o carries the write data with no check against reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl. The lock signals are completely absent from this logic.
evidence.supports_claim: Write enable (we_o) is asserted and write data is forwarded without any register-lock or access-control guard, allowing writes to RSA registers regardless of permission state.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates rsa_wrapper with reglk_ctrl[13*8+7:13*8] and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][13] (lines 1021-1022), showing intent to enforce register-lock and privilege-level access control. However, these are only connected to the wrapper's input ports and not propagated into the axi_lite_interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The system is designed with register-lock and access-control signals, but the enforcement point (axi_lite_interface) does not receive them, creating a gap in the permission chain.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl and acc_ctrl_c are defined (lines 214-216) as system-wide permission controls for 14 peripherals including RSA. Their presence confirms the intent to protect peripheral registers, but enforcement depends on each wrapper correctly gating write access.
evidence.supports_claim: Register lock and access control infrastructure exists but is not enforced at the write path within axi_lite_interface used by rsa_wrapper.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rsa_wrapper and similar wrappers (rng_wrapper, pkt_wrapper) all instantiate axi_lite_interface to handle AXI transactions. The axi_lite_interface module has no ports for reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl signals and unconditionally asserts we_o and passes write data when AXI write handshaking completes. While the wrapper modules receive register-lock and access-control inputs from riscv_peripherals, these signals are not connected to the axi_lite_interface instance. The result is that write transactions to RSA (and other sensitive) registers bypass the intended permission checks at the AXI interface layer.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or unauthorized AXI master can write to RSA cryptographic registers (including prime inputs, message inputs, control registers) regardless of the reglk_ctrl lock state or privilege level, potentially compromising cryptographic operations, extracting sensitive key material, or disrupting secure computations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the complete internal logic of rsa_wrapper where reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i might be used to gate en_acct or create a secondary check after the axi_lite_interface. It is possible that en_acct (distinct from en in SNIP-0013 line 37) applies additional gating downstream of the AXI interface, but this is not evidenced in the provided snippets. The write data path through we_o is the primary concern shown by available evidence.
